Read the adhoc-and-secrets documentation

`SRC="/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi"; echo "=== adhoc-and-secrets doc ==="; ls "$SRC/docs/shells/" | grep -i "secret\|adhoc"; echo "---"; sed -n '1,120p' "$SRC/docs/shells/adhoc-and-secrets.md" 2>/dev/null`

```text
=== adhoc-and-secrets doc ===
adhoc-and-secrets.md
---
# Adhoc & Secrets — User-Local Override Layer

This repo provides **six** user-local files where you can drop machine-specific
config without going through `chezmoi edit` / commit / apply. They form a
3-tier × 3-scope matrix that covers every "I want to override something"
need without forcing duplication between zsh and bash.

All six files are listed in [`.chezmoiignore.tmpl`](https://github.com/daviddwlee84/dotfiles/blob/main/.chezmoiignore.tmpl)
— chezmoi will never overwrite, track, or delete them.

## TL;DR — which file?

```
Need to override something?
│
├─ Is it a secret (API key / token / proxy)?
│   ├─ Both shells need it?       → ~/.shellrc.secrets        ← shared
│   ├─ zsh only (e.g. compinit)?  → ~/.config/zsh/secrets.zsh
│   └─ bash only?                 → ~/.config/bash/secrets.sh
│
└─ Not a secret (alias / export / experiment / binding)?
    ├─ POSIX, both shells?        → ~/.shellrc.adhoc          ← shared
    ├─ zsh-only construct?        → ~/.zshrc.adhoc
    │   (setopt, ZLE widget, compdef, bindkey, read -q, ${m:t}, glob qualifiers)
    └─ bash-only construct?       → ~/.bashrc.adhoc
        (bind -x, ble-bind, shopt, complete -F, OMB plugin tweaks)
```

**Default to the shared file.** Drop down to per-shell only when you actually
need shell-specific syntax.

## The six files

| Tier | File | Scope | Auto-stub | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Secrets | `~/.shellrc.secrets` | both shells (POSIX) | no | Sourced by both `~/.zshrc` and `~/.bashrc` before any adhoc files |
| Secrets | `~/.config/zsh/secrets.zsh` | zsh only | no | Sourced after the shared layer; can use zsh-only syntax |
| Secrets | `~/.config/bash/secrets.sh` | bash only | no | Same as above but bash |
| Adhoc | `~/.shellrc.adhoc` | both shells (POSIX) | yes | Auto-created on first shell launch with a POSIX-only warning header |
| Adhoc | `~/.zshrc.adhoc` | zsh only | yes | Auto-created on first zsh launch |
| Adhoc | `~/.bashrc.adhoc` | bash only | yes | Auto-created on first bash launch |

Why secrets aren't auto-stubbed: an empty `secrets.*` file is a footgun
(committed accidentally is harmless because it's chezmoi-ignored, but `set -u`
scripts could trip on missing vars and silently 'succeed' with `[ -r ]`
guards). You create them yourself the first time you have a secret to store.

## Load order

### zsh (`dot_zshrc.tmpl`)

```
1. Shared POSIX modules        ~/.config/shell/*.sh           (managed)
2. zsh modules                 ~/.config/zsh/*.zsh + tools/   (managed)
3. Shared secrets (NEW)        ~/.shellrc.secrets             (user-local)
4. zsh secrets                 ~/.config/zsh/secrets.zsh      (user-local)
5. Shared adhoc (NEW)          ~/.shellrc.adhoc               (user-local)
6. zsh adhoc                   ~/.zshrc.adhoc                 (user-local)
```

### bash (`dot_bashrc.tmpl`, mapped to its 12-step init order)

See [`docs/shells/bash.md`](bash.md) for the full step list. The relevant
positions:

```
... steps 1-7: env, exports, OMB, bash-preexec, ble.sh source, modules ...
 8. atuin init
 9a. Shared secrets (NEW)      ~/.shellrc.secrets    ← before ble-attach
 9b. bash secrets              ~/.config/bash/secrets.sh
10. ble-attach
11. ~/.bash_aliases + ~/.bashrc.d/*  (distro skel compat)
12a. Shared adhoc (NEW)        ~/.shellrc.adhoc      ← after ble-attach
12b. bash adhoc                ~/.bashrc.adhoc
```

Secrets run **before** `ble-attach` so vars are already exported when ble.sh,
starship, atuin, and OMB precmd hooks fire. Adhoc runs **after** `ble-attach`
so any `bind -x` / `ble-bind` calls in `~/.bashrc.adhoc` take effect (the
shared file should not contain ble-bind, but the position is the same for
symmetry).

## Precedence rules

Two simple rules, applied in order:

1. **Secrets sourced before adhoc.** This means `~/.shellrc.adhoc` can read
   variables defined in `~/.shellrc.secrets`. Useful pattern:

   ```sh
   # ~/.shellrc.secrets
   export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...

   # ~/.shellrc.adhoc
   alias gpt='openai api -k "$OPENAI_API_KEY" ...'
   ```

2. **Shared sourced before per-shell.** Per-shell files are sourced *last* in
   their tier, so they override the shared layer on any naming conflict —
   matching the existing `~/.bash_aliases` → `~/.bashrc.adhoc` ordering.

   ```sh
   # ~/.shellrc.adhoc
   export MY_VAR=shared

   # ~/.zshrc.adhoc
   export MY_VAR=zshlocal       # zsh sees this; bash sees `shared`
   ```

## POSIX-only constraint for shared files

`~/.shellrc.adhoc` and `~/.shellrc.secrets` are sourced by **both** shells, so
they MUST be POSIX-portable.

**Forbidden in shared files:**

- zsh-only: `setopt`, `compdef`, ZLE widgets (`zle -N`), `bindkey`, `read -q`,
  `${m:t}` modifier expansion, glob qualifiers (`*(.om[1])`), `(($var))` arith
  in some contexts, `=~` with PCRE
- bash-only: `bind -x`, `bind -X`, `ble-bind`, `shopt`, `complete -F`,
```
</details></tool-use>

If it's a new install **mechanism** (vs new tool through an existing mechanism), also update the relevant § Per-manager catalog subsection and § Decision tree | High-churn case is just the A–Z row (one line per tool). Reverse refs from both pages auto-link back. |
20:| `dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_fleet` (umbrella `fleet chezmoi <action>` namespace + top-level generic primitives `tmux`/`info`/`pueue`/`exec`/`hosts`/`edit`) / `scripts/fleet/` (`apply.py` / `tmux.py` / `info.py` / `pueue.py` / `exec.py` / `hosts.py`) / `justfile` `fleet` + `fleet-*` recipes / `dot_config/fleet/` / `dot_config/television/cable/fleet-hosts.toml` / sudo helper consumption / `dot_config/tmux/executable_tmux-session-summary.py` `--json` schema / `dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_pqsum` `json`/`ai --stdin-json` schemas / `scripts/fleet/exec.py` PATH-prelude + AIEXEC schema | [docs/this_repo/fleet-apply.md](docs/this_repo/fleet-apply.md), [docs/tools/pueue.md](docs/tools/pueue.md), [docs/tools/fleet-exec.md](docs/tools/fleet-exec.md), [docs/tools/fleet-hosts.md](docs/tools/fleet-hosts.md) | The `--json` schema emitted by `tmux-session-summary.py` and the raw-fallback parser in `scripts/fleet/tmux.py:_parse_raw` MUST stay in sync — adding a field to `Session`/`Window` dataclass means updating both. The PATH-augmentation prelude in `scripts/fleet/pueue.py:_REMOTE_CMD` and `scripts/fleet/exec.py:_PATH_PRELUDE` MUST stay in sync — both rely on the same chezmoi → cargo → uv → ~/bin → brew → linuxbrew order (deliberately diverges from the user's interactive shell PATH so package-manager dirs win over legacy ~/bin). |

Change one → change the other in the same commit. | Shared helpers (`open_id`/`copy_id`/`fetch_histories`/`detect_kind`/`make_client`) live in `scripts/mlf/__init__.py` — refactor there, not in the call sites. 24:| New in-house CLI in `dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_*` (or new subcommand on existing one) | Add tab completion for both shells per the decision flow in `docs/zsh/zsh-completions.md` Section F; **two files** if hand-written (`dot_config/zsh/tools/<NN>_<name>_completion.zsh` + `dot_config/bash/<NN>_<name>_completion.bash`) — keep them in sync; add row to that Section F table and to `docs/shells/aliases.md` Package Managers section if the CLI ships an `<tool>-update-completion` helper | [docs/zsh/zsh-completions.md](docs/zsh/zsh-completions.md) Section F. Existing patterns: `mi-router` (Strategy A, tyro self-gen + mtime invalidation in `dot_config/shell/47_mi_router.sh`), `fleet`/`mlf`/`pqsum`/`x` (Strategy B, hand-written eager-load in `dot_config/{zsh/tools,bash}/45-49_*_completion.*`). Dynamic candidates (host names, group names) shell out to the CLI itself — `_fleet_hosts_one` calls `fleet hosts --list`. |
25:| Tool inventory / in-house CLIs / `tv` channels / keymaps **surfaced to agents** (new prompt key or `executable_*` CLI) | `dot_agents/skills/chezmoi-dotfiles/SKILL.md.tmpl` — the per-host chezmoi-templated agent-facing index (→ `~/.agents/skills/`, symlinked into `~/.claude/skills/`). Re-renders every `chezmoi apply` so freshness is automatic; only edit it to gate a section on a **new prompt key** or name a stable new CLI. | [docs/tools/agent-skills.md](docs/tools/agent-skills.md) § First-party templated skill. Keep it lean/self-discovering (pointers over enumerations). `.chezmoiignore` re-include needs single-`*` globs — a bare `**` no-ops the `!`. |

The hook `.chezmoiscripts/global/run_after_50_generate_completions.sh.tmpl` then auto-regenerates on every `chezmoi apply` (binary-mtime check, idempotent — ~21ms when cached). | [docs/zsh/zsh-completions.md](docs/zsh/zsh-completions.md) Section A inventory + "Generating Completions After Fresh Install" section. Currently 14 tools auto-generated: chezmoi/mise/uv/just/starship/gh/docker/rg/fd/bat/delta/zellij/pueue/opencode. NOT auto-generated (handled elsewhere): sesh/tv/worktrunk (shell-startup mtime check), bw/marimo/thefuck/try-cli (cached eval), mi-router (its own startup script + tyro `#compdef` rewrite), fleet/mlf/pqsum/x (hand-written). |
28:| AI agent autodetect order or `AICAP_*_MODEL` defaults | Edit **only** [`dot_config/shell/04_ai_agents.sh`](dot_config/shell/04_ai_agents.sh) — the SSOT. Both shells source it; the **four** Python consumers (`dot_config/tmux/executable_tmux-session-summary.py`, `scripts/aiblock.py`, `dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_pqsum`, `scripts/fleet/exec.py`) regex-parse the same file for cron / tmux-popup contexts where env isn't inherited. Each agent's `-m`/`--model` flag is conditional on its env var being non-empty so a retired model ID falls through to the CLI's own default. When adding a NEW agent, update: SSOT (one new `AICAP_<AGENT>_MODEL` line), `04_ai_capture.sh:_aiagent_invoke` case, **all four** Python `AGENT_CONFIG` dicts. **Four consumers = pain threshold reached** — the extraction TODO (`scripts/aisum/__init__.py`) is now blocking ergonomics; next AI-tooling change should land that refactor before adding more callsites. | — |

 `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_ansible/roles/llm_tools/tasks/main.yml`

```text
1	---
2	# Local LLM tools
3	# - litellm: uv tool install
4	# - llmfit: Homebrew on macOS, official local installer on Linux
5	# - models: Homebrew when available, cargo fallback on Linux
6	# - ollama: Homebrew formula on macOS unless Brewfile GUI install is enabled
7	#            OR an ollama CLI is already present (e.g. the ollama-app cask, which
8	#            ships /usr/local/bin/ollama and would otherwise break `brew link`);
9	#            official installer on Linux when sudo/root is available
10	
11	- name: Ensure ~/.local/bin exists for local LLM tools
12	  ansible.builtin.file:
13	    path: "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/bin"
14	    state: directory
15	    mode: '0755'
16	
17	- name: Install LiteLLM via uv
18	  ansible.builtin.command: >-
19	    uv tool install {{ item.name }}
20	    {% if item.python is defined %}--python {{ item.python }} {% endif %}
21	    {% if item.with is defined %}{% for dep in item.with %}--with {{ dep }} {% endfor %}{% endif %}
22	  args:
23	    creates: "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/bin/{{ item.binary }}"
24	  loop: "{{ llm_tools_uv }}"
25	  loop_control:
26	    label: "{{ item.name }}"
27	
28	- name: Install llmfit via Homebrew (macOS)
29	  when: ansible_facts["os_family"] == "Darwin"
30	  community.general.homebrew:
31	    name: llmfit
32	    state: present
33	
34	- name: Install llmfit via official local installer (Linux)
35	  when: ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
36	  ansible.builtin.shell: |
37	    curl -fsSL https://llmfit.axjns.dev/install.sh | sh -s -- --local
38	  args:
39	    creates: "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/bin/llmfit"
40	  failed_when: false
41	  register: llmfit_install_result
42	
43	- name: Warn llmfit installation failed
44	  when:
45	    - llmfit_install_result is defined
46	    - llmfit_install_result.rc is defined
47	    - llmfit_install_result.rc != 0
48	  ansible.builtin.debug:
49	    msg: "llmfit installation failed (likely network timeout) - skipping"
50	
51	- name: Check if Homebrew is available
52	  when: ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
53	  ansible.builtin.command: which brew
54	  register: llm_tools_brew_check
55	  changed_when: false
56	  failed_when: false
57	
58	- name: Install models via Homebrew (macOS)
59	  when: ansible_facts["os_family"] == "Darwin"
60	  community.general.homebrew:
61	    name: models
62	    state: present
63	
64	- name: Check if models formula is available in Homebrew (Linuxbrew)
65	  when:
66	    - ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
67	    - llm_tools_brew_check.rc == 0
68	  ansible.builtin.command: brew info models
69	  register: llm_tools_models_brew_info
70	  changed_when: false
71	  failed_when: false
72	
73	- name: Install models via Homebrew (Linuxbrew)
74	  when:
75	    - ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
76	    - llm_tools_brew_check.rc == 0
77	    - llm_tools_models_brew_info.rc == 0
78	  community.general.homebrew:
79	    name: models
80	    state: present
81	
82	- name: Install models via cargo fallback (Linux)
83	  when:
84	    - ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
85	    - llm_tools_brew_check.rc != 0 or (llm_tools_models_brew_info is defined and llm_tools_models_brew_info.rc != 0)
86	  ansible.builtin.command: cargo install modelsdev
87	  args:
88	    creates: "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.cargo/bin/models"
89	  environment:
90	    PATH: "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/share/mise/shims:{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.cargo/bin:{{ ansible_facts['env']['PATH'] }}"
91	
92	- name: Check whether an ollama CLI is already present (macOS)
93	  # The ollama-app cask (GUI runtime) installs /usr/local/bin/ollama as a symlink
94	  # into Ollama.app. Installing the CLI-only formula on top of it makes `brew link`
95	  # collide on that path and abort the play. If any ollama is already on PATH, the
96	  # user already has a working CLI — skip the formula instead of fighting it.
97	  when: ansible_facts["os_family"] == "Darwin"
98	  ansible.builtin.command: which ollama
99	  register: llm_tools_ollama_macos_check
100	  changed_when: false
101	  failed_when: false
102	
103	- name: Install Ollama via Homebrew formula (macOS CLI install)
104	  when:
105	    - ansible_facts["os_family"] == "Darwin"
106	    - not (llm_tools_install_brew_apps | bool)
107	    - llm_tools_ollama_macos_check.rc != 0
108	  community.general.homebrew:
109	    name: ollama
110	    state: present
111	
112	- name: Check if Ollama is installed (Linux)
113	  when: ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
114	  ansible.builtin.command: ollama --version
115	  register: llm_tools_ollama_linux_check
116	  changed_when: false
117	  failed_when: false
118	
119	- name: Warn that Ollama is skipped in noRoot mode
120	  when:
121	    - ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
122	    - llm_tools_no_root | bool
123	    - llm_tools_ollama_linux_check.rc != 0
124	  ansible.builtin.debug:
125	    msg: "Skipping Ollama in noRoot mode. Install it later with sudo/root if you want the local runtime service."
126	
127	- name: Install Ollama via official installer (Linux)
128	  when:
129	    - ansible_facts["os_family"] in ["Debian", "RedHat"]
130	    - not (llm_tools_no_root | bool)
131	    - llm_tools_ollama_linux_check.rc != 0
132	  become: true
133	  tags: [sudo]
134	  ansible.builtin.shell: |
135	    curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
136	  args:
137	    creates: /usr/local/bin/ollama
138	  failed_when: false
139	  register: ollama_install_result
140	
141	- name: Warn ollama installation failed
142	  when:
143	    - ollama_install_result is defined
144	    - ollama_install_result.rc is defined
145	    - ollama_install_result.rc != 0
146	  ansible.builtin.debug:
147	    msg: "ollama installation failed (likely network timeout) - skipping"
148
```
</details></tool-use>

`SP="$(chezmoi source-path)"; echo "############ tv-vs-fzf.md ############"; cat "$SP/docs/tools/tv-vs-fzf.md"; echo; echo "=== gum usage in scripts ==="; grep -rniE "\bgum \b|gum choose|gum filter|gum input|gum confirm" "$SP/dot_dotfiles" "$SP/dot_config/shell" "$SP/dot_config/zsh" 2>/dev/null | head`

```text
############ tv-vs-fzf.md ############
# tv vs fzf — Comparison & Best Practices

Two fuzzy finders, two philosophies. This dotfiles repo uses **both**.

| | **fzf** | **television (tv)** |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| Philosophy | Low-level fuzzy primitive; you compose workflows with shell glue | Structured picker framework; workflows declared as TOML channels |
| Analogy | Unix pipe building block | Terminal-native Telescope.nvim |
| Matcher | Custom mature engine (multi-year, battle-tested) | [nucleo](https://github.com/helix-editor/nucleo) (shared with Helix) |
| Query syntax | fuzzy, exact (`'`), prefix (`^`), suffix (`$`), inverse (`!`), **OR** (`\|`) | fuzzy, exact, prefix, suffix, substring, negate — no OR |
| Extensibility | Shell scripts, env vars, `--bind`, `--preview`, `--reload` | TOML channel files in `cable/` directory |
| Shell integration | `Ctrl+R` / `Ctrl+T` / `Alt+C` + `**<TAB>` completion | `Ctrl+R` / `Ctrl+T` with context-aware channel triggers |
| Sorting control | `--tiebreak`, `--sort`, `--no-sort` | `no_sort`, `frecency` per channel |
| Multi-select | `--multi` + Tab/Shift-Tab | Built-in |
| Preview | `--preview` flag (any command) | `[preview]` section in channel TOML |
| Actions on select | `--bind 'enter:execute(...)'` | `[actions]` section; fork/execute modes |
| Ecosystem maturity | Huge: vim, tmux, zsh, bash, fish, git, kubectl, etc. | Growing; fewer third-party integrations |
| Config in this repo | `dot_config/zsh/tools/10_fzf.zsh` | `dot_config/television/cable/*.toml` |

## When to use which (in this dotfiles repo)

| Use case | Tool | Why |
|----------|------|-----|
| Shell history (`Ctrl+R`) | fzf | More sorting modes, `FZF_CTRL_R_OPTS` customization |
| File picker (`Ctrl+T`) | fzf | Deep completion integration, `**<TAB>` |
| Directory cd (`Alt+C`) | fzf | Built-in, simple |
| Tab completion preview | fzf | `_fzf_comprun` per-command previews |
| Sesh session picker | tv | Channel with source cycling (tmux/zoxide/config/fd), preview, kill action |
| CLI tools picker | tv | Channel parsing `cli-tools.md`, preview with tldr |
| Aliases picker | tv | Channel listing all runtime aliases/functions |
| Git branch switching | tv | Channel with preview + checkout action |
| Custom domain pickers | tv | Declarative TOML channels are faster to author than shell wrappers |

## "Telescope-like workflow" explained

The pattern (popularized by [telescope.nvim](https://github.com/nvim-telescope/telescope.nvim)):

```
Source → Filter/Sort → Preview → Action
```

You use the **same picker UI** with **different data sources**. Each picker (channel) defines what to search, how to preview, and what actions are available. You don't switch mental models — just switch channels.

tv implements this natively: each `.toml` file in `cable/` is a channel recipe.

## Channel / Cable Best Practices

### 1. One channel = one job

Don't build a mega-channel. Keep each channel focused:

- `files` — find files
- `text` — grep content
- `git-branches` — switch branches
- `dirs` — cd via zoxide/fd
- `history` — shell history
- `tools` — CLI tool reference

### 2. Source should be clean and stable

The `[source]` runs a shell command. Keep it:

- **Machine-friendly output** — one entry per line, predictable format
- **Lightweight** — no heavy formatting; let `display` / `output` / `preview` handle presentation
- **Use source cycling** for variants of the same thing (e.g. `fd -t f` vs `fd -t f -H` for hidden files)

Rule: only cycle sources that share the same mental model. "Files vs hidden files" = good. "Files vs docker containers" = bad.

### 3. Preview is the workflow's soul

Good preview transforms the experience from "pick blindly" to "browse with context":

| Channel | Preview command |
|---------|----------------|
| files | `bat --color=always {}` |
| text/grep | `bat --color=always --highlight-line {line} {file}` |
| git branches | `git log --oneline --graph {}` |
| git log | `git show {}` |
| docker images | `docker inspect {} \| jq` |
| tools | `tldr {}` or `{} --help` |

### 4. Keep actions minimal and consistent

Per channel, define:

| Key | Role | Example |
|-----|------|---------|
| `Enter` | Primary action | Open, connect, execute |
| `Ctrl+E` | Edit | `$EDITOR {}` |
| `Ctrl+Y` | Yank/copy | Copy to clipboard |
| `Ctrl+X` | Destructive | Kill session, delete |
| `Ctrl+/` | Toggle preview | Built-in |

### 5. Respect original ordering for temporal data

For shell history, git log, and similar temporal sources:

- Set `no_sort = true` to preserve source order
- Set `frecency = false` to disable frequency-based ranking

You want "most recent commands" not "highest fuzzy score from 6 months ago".

### 6. Use shell integration triggers

tv's shell integration can detect the current command prefix and auto-select a channel:

```toml
# In shell_integration config
[channel_triggers]
"git checkout" = "git-branches"
"git switch"   = "git-branches"
"cd"           = "dirs"
"ls"           = "dirs"
"vim"          = "files"
"nvim"         = "files"
"cat"          = "files"
"bat"          = "files"
"export"       = "env"
"unset"        = "env"
```

This turns the shell prompt into a **context-aware picker entry point**.

## Cable organization

### By data type (recommended for most people)

```
cable/
├── files.toml
├── text.toml
├── dirs.toml
├── git-branches.toml
├── git-log.toml
├── env.toml
├── history.toml
├── sesh.toml
└── tools.toml
```

### By work context (for project-heavy workflows)

```
cable/
├── code-files.toml
├── code-grep.toml
├── code-symbols.toml
├── repo-switch.toml
├── notes.toml
├── infra-docker.toml
└── infra-k8s.toml
```

## Practical comparison table

| Scenario | fzf approach | tv approach |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Find project files | `fd -t f \| fzf --preview 'bat {}'` | `tv files` (built-in channel) |
| Grep repo content | `rg --line-number foo \| fzf --preview 'bat ...'` | `tv text` (built-in channel) |
| Switch git branch | `git branch \| fzf \| xargs git checkout` | `tv git-branches` → Enter checks out |
| Shell history | `Ctrl+R` (fzf built-in, toggle sort with `Ctrl+R`) | `Ctrl+R` (tv shell integration) |
| Tab completion | `ssh **<TAB>`, `kill **<TAB>`, custom `_fzf_complete_*` | Not available — fzf only |
| Session management | `sesh list \| fzf-tmux` (Alt+S in this repo) | `tv sesh` with source cycling (prefix+T) |
| URL picker in tmux | `prefix + u` (tmux-fzf-url) | N/A — fzf plugin |
| Custom tool lookup | Alt+T (fzf ZLE widget) | `tv tools` / `tv aliases` |

## Summary

> **fzf** = mature infrastructure primitive. Unmatched shell integration depth, query syntax, and ecosystem.
>
> **tv** = modern workflow framework. Better out-of-box channel/preview/action packaging, less shell glue needed.
>
> Fuzzy matching quality: comparable. Query syntax: fzf has more operators (notably OR). Shell completion: fzf only.

Use both. Let fzf own shell-native integration (`Ctrl+R`, `Ctrl+T`, `Alt+C`, `**<TAB>`). Let tv own structured pickers where channels shine (sesh, tools, aliases, domain-specific workflows).

## Community channels vs our custom channels

tv ships a large set of [community-maintained channels](https://alexpasmantier.github.io/television/community/channels-unix) covering files, dirs, env, git (branch/log/diff/stash/tags/worktrees/remotes/submodules), docker (containers/images/volumes/networks/compose), k8s, brew, cargo, GitHub PRs/issues, systemd journal, and more.

Install/update community channels with:

```bash
tv update-channels
```

This downloads channels into `~/.config/television/cable/`, skipping channels whose requirements aren't met on your system and skipping channels that already exist (won't overwrite your custom ones).

### Do we reinvent the wheel?

| Our channel | Community equivalent | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| `sesh.toml` | `sesh.toml` (community) | **Retired** — community caught up with identical logic. Our version is kept as `sesh.toml.reference` for reference; `tv update-channels` provides the active one. |
| `tools.toml` | None | **Unique** — parses our `cli-tools.md`, no equivalent |
| `aliases.toml` | `alias.toml` | **Superset** — community version is minimal (`$SHELL -ic 'alias'`, no functions, no actions). Ours adds functions via `typeset -f`, formatted columns, execute action, copy-to-clipboard |

**Conclusion: minimal duplication.** `sesh.toml` was retired in favor of the community version. The other two custom channels have no equivalent or significantly extend the community version.

### Community channels worth adopting

After running `tv update-channels`, these are already available (no manual setup needed). Particularly useful ones:

| Channel | What it does |
|---------|-------------|
| `git-branch` | Branch picker with checkout/delete/merge/rebase actions |
| `git-log` | Commit log with cherry-pick/revert/checkout |
| `git-stash` | Stash browser with apply/pop/drop |
| `git-diff` | Changed files with stage/restore/edit |
| `brew-packages` | Homebrew formula/cask list with upgrade/uninstall |
| `docker-containers` | Container management with start/stop/logs/exec/remove |
| `docker-images` | Image picker with run/shell/remove |
| `dirs` | Directory picker via fd (with hidden toggle via source cycling) |
| `files` | File picker via fd + bat preview (with hidden toggle) |
| `env` | Environment variable browser |
| `dotfiles` | Browse `~/.config` files with bat preview + edit action |
| `gh-issues` / `gh-prs` | GitHub issue/PR picker with rich preview |
| `just-recipes` | Justfile recipe picker with preview + execute |

To write a custom channel, create a `.toml` file in `dot_config/television/cable/` (deployed via chezmoi). Custom channels won't be overwritten by `tv update-channels`. See the [channel spec](https://alexpasmantier.github.io/television/reference/channel-spec) for TOML format.

## See also

- [fzf quick reference](fzf.md)
- [tv quick reference](tv.md)
- [keyboard shortcuts](../keyboard-shortcuts.md)

=== gum usage in scripts ===

/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/shell/29_media.sh:107:                w=$(gum input --placeholder "max width px (e.g. 1280)" --value 1280) || return 0
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/shell/04_ai_capture.sh`

```text
240	    http)
241	      # OpenAI-compatible chat-completions over curl. Covers OpenRouter (with
242	      # API key) and local Ollama (no auth) with the same code path. Never
243	      # auto-detected — opt in via AICAP_AGENT=http or `-a http`.
244	      local url="$AICAP_HTTP_URL" model="$AICAP_HTTP_MODEL"
245	      local key="${AICAP_HTTP_API_KEY:-${OPENROUTER_API_KEY:-}}"
246	      if ! command -v jq &>/dev/null; then
247	        printf '%s\n' "aifix: jq is required for the http agent" >&2
248	        return 1
249	      fi
250	      if ! command -v curl &>/dev/null; then
251	        printf '%s\n' "aifix: curl is required for the http agent" >&2
252	        return 1
253	      fi
254	      # Extract host from URL. Original used zsh-nested `${${url#*://}%%/*}`;
255	      # bash doesn't support nested expansion, so do it in two steps.
256	      local host
257	      host="${url#*://}"
258	      host="${host%%/*}"
259	      [ "$AICAP_SHOW_METADATA" != "1" ] && printf '%s\n' "http ($model @ $host)" >&2
260	      _aicap_spinner_start "http $model…"
261	      local payload response
262	      payload=$(jq -n --arg m "$model" --arg p "$prompt" \
263	        '{model: $m, messages: [{role: "user", content: $p}], stream: false}')
264	      local -a curl_args
265	      curl_args=(-sS -X POST "$url" -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d "$payload")
266	      [ -n "$key" ] && curl_args+=(-H "Authorization: Bearer $key")
267	      response=$(curl "${curl_args[@]}")
268	      rc=$?
269	      _aicap_spinner_stop
270	      if (( rc != 0 )); then
271	        printf '%s\n' "http: curl failed (rc=$rc) — check AICAP_HTTP_URL ($url)" >&2
272	        return $rc
273	      fi
274	      local err
275	      err=$(printf '%s\n' "$response" | jq -r '.error.message // (.error | strings) // empty' 2>/dev/null)
276	      if [ -n "$err" ]; then
277	        printf '%s\n' "http: API error: $err" >&2
278	        return 1
279	      fi
280	      reply=$(printf '%s\n' "$response" | jq -r '.choices[0].message.content // empty')
281	      if [ -z "$reply" ]; then
282	        printf '%s\n' "http: empty reply (raw response: $response)" >&2
283	        return 1
284	      fi
285	      if [ "$AICAP_SHOW_METADATA" = "1" ]; then
286	        tokens_in=$(printf '%s\n' "$response" | jq -r '.usage.prompt_tokens // "?"')
287	        tokens_out=$(printf '%s\n' "$response" | jq -r '.usage.completion_tokens // "?"')
288	        printf '%s\n' "http ($model @ $host) | in=$tokens_in out=$tokens_out" >&2
289	      fi
```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-09 11:07:28Z)**_

I have a comprehensive picture. Here is my structured report.

---

# Environment Report: `translate-cli-tui` host

## 1. Project directory — near-empty, freshly scaffolded
`/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-translate-cli-tui/` contains **no source code yet**. Only:
- `.git/` — initialized repo, **no commits** (empty `refs/heads`, empty objects/pack), default branch not yet created.
- `.claude/` — `settings.json`, `settings.local.json`, and empty `plans/`.
- `.specstory/` — `.project.json`, `cli/config.toml`, and one history file `history/2026-07-09_11-04-39Z-cli-tui-typo-framework.md`.

Truly greenfield — nothing to migrate or conflict with.

## 2. Language runtimes (exist / version / path)
| Runtime | Status | Version | Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| Python | ✅ | 3.13.3 | `/Users/david/.local/bin/python3` |
| uv | ✅ | 0.9.24 | `/Users/david/.local/bin/uv` |
| Rust (rustc) | ✅ | 1.96.0 | `/Users/david/.cargo/bin/rustc` (rustup) |
| Cargo | ✅ | 1.96.0 | `/Users/david/.cargo/bin/cargo` |
| Go | ✅ | 1.26.4 | mise: `~/.local/share/mise/installs/go/1.26.4/bin/go` |
| Node | ✅ | 24.13.1 | mise: `~/.local/share/mise/installs/node/24.13.1/bin/node` |
| Bun | ✅ | 1.3.9 | mise: `~/.local/share/mise/installs/bun/1.3.9/bin/bun` |
| **pipx** | ❌ MISSING | — | (use `uv tool` instead) |
| **Deno** | ❌ MISSING | — | — |

Note: Go/Node/Bun are **mise-managed** (`~/.local/share/mise/installs/...`), so versions are pinnable per-project via mise. Python and uv are standalone in `~/.local/bin`.

## 3. Package managers / installers
- **Homebrew** ✅ 6.0.8 at `/usr/local/bin/brew` (Intel prefix — note `go version` reports `darwin/amd64`, this is an x86_64 / Rosetta-style toolchain, not arm64).
- **uv** ✅ — both project envs (`uv venv`/`uv run`) and global tools (`uv tool`). This is the de-facto pipx replacement here. Currently-installed uv tools include: `litellm`, `mlflow`, `jupyterlab`, `marimo`, `ansible-core`, `mihome-ctl`, `apprise`, `copyparty`, `pre-commit`, `git-filter-repo`.
- **cargo install** ✅ — `~/.cargo/bin` currently holds only `trunk` (WASM bundler) and `recon` beyond the rustup shims; no TUI crates pre-built (expected — Rust libs are per-project).
- **npm/bun** ✅ available; no relevant global JS packages installed (no ink/oclif/commander/blessed globally).

## 4. Terminal environment (TUI-relevant)
- `TERM=xterm-256color`, **`COLORTERM=truecolor`** → **24-bit truecolor confirmed**.
- `SHELL=/bin/zsh`; `LANG=LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8` → full UTF-8 (matters for CJK/translation glyphs).
- Process chain: `claude ← specstory ← zsh ← herdr` — the session runs under **`herdr`** (a headless session/multiplexer), so the GUI terminal emulator isn't in the process tree and `TERM_PROGRAM` is empty. However, **modern truecolor emulators are installed as casks**: `alacritty`, `wezterm`, `warp` (no ghostty/kitty/iterm2). Also installed: multiplexers `tmux`, `zellij`, `sesh`, `workmux`, `herdr`.
- Bottom line: modern, truecolor, UTF-8 terminal — safe to target rich colors, Unicode, and mouse.

## 5. Existing CLI/TUI frameworks
- **Python:** ❌ None of textual / rich / prompt_toolkit / typer / click are installed in the base `python3` (only 3 packages total). Trivial to add via `uv` though — zero friction.
- **Rust:** ❌ No ratatui/clap pre-built (per-project only). Toolchain ready.
- **Go:** ❌ No bubbletea/cobra pre-built and `~/go/bin` is empty — BUT see below, the Charm *CLI tool* ecosystem is heavily present.
- **Node:** ❌ No ink/oclif globally.

So no framework is *pre-installed as a library*, but every runtime needed to add one is present.

## 6. Interactive-selection / TUI binaries (the decisive signal)
| Binary | Status | Path |
|---|---|---|
| `fzf` | ✅ | `/usr/local/bin/fzf` |
| `gum` (Charm) | ✅ | `/usr/local/bin/gum` |
| `tv` / `television` | ✅ | `/usr/local/bin/tv` |
| `glow` (Charm) | ✅ | `/usr/local/bin/glow` |
| `bat` | ✅ | `/usr/local/bin/bat` |
| **`trans` (translate-shell)** | ❌ **MISSING** | — |

Additional Charm-stack tooling in brew leaves: **`vhs`, `freeze`** (plus `gum`, `glow`, `television`) — this machine clearly leans into the **Charm / Bubble Tea** aesthetic. Also present and relevant if translation is LLM-backed: `litellm` (uv tool), `gemini-cli`, `models`, `ollama` (cask + app), and the `claude` cask. **No dedicated translation engine** (`trans`, no Python `deep-translator`/`argos`/`googletrans`) exists — a translation backend (API or LLM) will need to be chosen/added.

---

## Recommendation — most "batteries-included for smooth TUI"

**Top pick: Python 3.13 + Textual (+ Rich), bootstrapped via `uv`.**
- Rationale: Textual is the most batteries-included TUI framework (widgets, CSS-like styling, async event loop, built-in truecolor + mouse), and `uv` makes a reproducible env instant with zero global pollution — the host already uses `uv tool` as its Python distribution mechanism. Terminal is truecolor + UTF-8, so Textual will render at full fidelity. LLM translation backends are one `uv add` away (`litellm` already proven on this box).

**Strong runner-up / house style: Go 1.26 + Bubble Tea + Lip Gloss.** The machine is saturated with the Charm ecosystem (`gum`, `glow`, `vhs`, `freeze`, `television`), so a Bubble Tea app fits the existing tooling culture and gives a single static binary. More manual (Elm-architecture boilerplate) than Textual, hence second.

**Third: Rust + ratatui + clap.** Toolchain is ready (note: x86_64 build) but it's immediate-mode and the most assembly-required for a rich interactive UI.

Quick-win alternative if you want a shell-glue prototype first: compose installed binaries — `fzf`/`gum`/`tv` for interactive language pick + an LLM/API call — no framework needed, though it caps how "smooth" the final TUI can get.

One gap to plan for regardless of stack: **there is no translation engine installed** (`trans` absent, no Python translate libs), so decide early between an online API vs. local LLM (`ollama` present) vs. `litellm`.

```text
=== clean translate CLI/alias/function check (source dirs only) ===
/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/homebrew/Brewfile.darwin.tmpl:172:# - Immersive Translate (browser extension)

/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/zsh/tools/05_aisuggest.zsh:87:typeset -g _AISUGGEST_BUILTIN_PROMPT='Translate natural language to a single shell command.
--- end ---

=== python_uv_tools defaults (how a uv tool binary is declared) ===
---
# Python CLI tools installed via uv tool install
# Each tool has:
#   - name: package name (can include extras like [cli])
#   - binary: executable name to check for idempotency
#   - with: (optional) additional dependency to install with --with
#   - with_executables_from: (optional) list of packages to install AND
#     expose their entry points in ~/.local/bin (e.g. jupyter-core to get
#     the `jupyter` meta-CLI; `notebook` to get `jupyter-notebook`)
#     NOTE: requires uv >= 0.8.5 (--with-executables-from added in that release)
#     For the meta-CLI dispatch model + the 8 jupyter binaries this exposes,
#     see docs/tools/notebooks.md "The `jupyter` meta-CLI vs standalone binaries".
#     For the auto-upgrade dispatch when uv is too old, see docs/this_repo/uv-bootstrap.md.
#   - extra_binaries: (optional) list of additional binary names that must
#     also exist in ~/.local/bin for the install to be considered complete.
#     Use this whenever `with_executables_from` is set, so adding a new
#     entry-point package later triggers a `--force` reinstall on machines
#     that already have `binary` present from a prior apply. Without this,
#     ansible's `creates:` guard short-circuits the install command and the
#     new exec shims are never written. See
#     pitfalls/uv-tool-install-creates-guard-misses-executables-from.md.
#   - python: (optional) pin Python version (e.g. "3.11" for thefuck)
#   - needs_modern_gcc: (optional, default false) tool transitively pulls
#     numpy 2.x / pandas 3.x / pyarrow which require GCC ≥ 9.3 to compile
#     from source. Skipped on systems where GCC < 9 (e.g. CentOS 7's
#     gcc 4.8.5). uv falls back to source build because numpy 2.x
#     dropped manylinux_2_17 wheels — see
#     pitfalls/centos7-numpy-pandas-source-build.md.

python_uv_tools:
  - name: thefuck
    binary: thefuck
    python: "3.11"  # Python 3.13 removed distutils which thefuck still uses
  - name: apprise
    binary: apprise
  - name: sqlit-tui[ssh]
    binary: sqlit
    needs_modern_gcc: true  # textual-fastdatatable -> pandas -> numpy 2.x
    with:
      - psycopg2-binary
      - pymysql
      - mssql-python
      - pyodbc
      - duckdb
  - name: python-dotenv[cli]
    binary: dotenv
  - name: git-filter-repo
    binary: git-filter-repo
  - name: mlflow
    binary: mlflow
    needs_modern_gcc: true  # sqlalchemy -> greenlet C++ ext needs proper C++11 (CentOS 7 gcc 4.8.5 lacks it by default)
  - name: tmuxp
    binary: tmuxp
  - name: visidata
    binary: vd
    needs_modern_gcc: true  # explicit pandas + pyarrow deps
    with:
      - pandas
      - pyarrow  # Required for parquet/feather/arrow loaders. Note: .feather
                 # files whose schema metadata marks columns as pandas
```
</details></tool-use>

`SP="$(chezmoi source-path)"; echo "=== yth launcher: config/data dir handling ==="; grep -nE "config|toml|user_(config|data)|platformdirs|XDG" "$SP/dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_yth" | head -20; echo; echo "=== how bin dir gets on PATH (exports) ==="; grep -nE "dotfiles/bin|\.local/bin|PATH" "$SP/dot_config/shell/00_exports.sh.tmpl" | head -20`

```text
=== yth launcher: config/data dir handling ===
8:#   "platformdirs>=4",
30:  play <id>                      mpv if configured+installed, else browser.
32:Data lives in a local sqlite DB (platformdirs user_data_dir/yth/history.db).
33:Cookie source + subtitle langs are configured in user_config_dir/yth/config.toml.
36:with the `tv yth` channel (dot_config/television/cable/yth.toml).

=== how bin dir gets on PATH (exports) ===
21:# PATH additions
22:# ~/.dotfiles/bin: chezmoi-managed scripts (this repo's dot_dotfiles/bin/)
25:# ~/.local/bin: auto-installed tools (uv tool, cargo install, mise, npm -g, ...)
26:export PATH="$HOME/.dotfiles/bin:$HOME/bin:$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
31:    export PATH="/snap/bin:$PATH"
37:# --no-modify-path` everywhere as of 2026-05). Keep PATH under dotfiles
42:    export PATH="$HOME/.opencode/bin:$PATH"
47:# Only prepend if the directory exists, so we don't pollute PATH on hosts
50:    export PATH="$HOME/.dotnet/tools:$PATH"
53:# system locations already on $PATH).
55:    export PATH="$HOME/.dotnet:$PATH"
```
</details></tool-use>

The dominant pattern across these tools is Python single-file launchers using PEP 723 inline script metadata run through uv, with tyro as the CLI framework (except for a couple using argparse), rich for output formatting, textual for TUI dashboards, and platformdirs for XDG-compliant config paths. Each launcher sits in the dotfiles bin directory as a thin dispatcher that routes to shared script modules, keeping the structure clean and maintainable.

For configuration, I'm standardizing on TOML files stored in `~/.config/<tool>/` with a precedence chain of CLI flags overriding config files, which override environment variables, which override interactive prompts. Data and cache directories follow XDG conventions via platformdirs. The deployment strategy is straightforward: chezmoi places executables into `~/.dotfiles/bin/` with proper permissions, and that directory gets prepended to PATH so tools are immediately available. For tools that should also exist as standalone uv binaries, I'm adding them to the Ansible role configuration so they get installed both ways.

Cross-file documentation requirements include tab completion for both zsh and bash, a dedicated tools markdown file, optional television channel configuration, updates to the tool-managers index, and aliases documentation. Television channels are a good fit for translation history—mirroring the yth pattern with TSV output, fuzzy matching via nucleo, and preview/action bindings. Deep searching still needs a separate CLI subcommand with FTS5 since television can't do live server-side queries. For shell integration, fzf handles the native keybindings like history and file completion.

For translation specifically, there's no existing tooling beyond the browser extension and an AI widget that converts natural language to shell commands. The linting setup uses ruff with defaults (no pre-commit enforcement), and Python 3.11+ is required across all launchers.

# LLM Infrastructure in David's chezmoi Dotfiles

**Repo root (chezmoi source):** `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi` · profile `macos_intel` · `installLlmTools=true`

The centerpiece of this user's LLM setup is a locally-running **GitHub Copilot → OpenAI/Anthropic proxy** (`copilot-proxy`). It is the single most important integration target: it's OpenAI-compatible, needs no API key, and is **currently running** exposing gpt-5.5, Claude, and Gemini models. Details below.

## 1. copilot-proxy (the primary integration target)

**What it is:** A local reverse-engineered proxy that lets a **GitHub Copilot subscription** back arbitrary OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible clients. It is **not** the user's own code — it's the npm package `@jeffreycao/copilot-api` (the maintained `caozhiyuan/copilot-api` fork), run on demand. What the user *built* is the **shell orchestration layer** around it.

- **Language:** TypeScript/JavaScript, run on the **Bun** runtime (`bunx`). Not installed by ansible — pulled on demand via `bunx`.
- **Package (pinned):** `@jeffreycao/copilot-api@1.13.14` (env `COPILOT_API_PKG`). The old original `copilot-api@0.7.0` is also supported.
- **Launch/management:** Shell functions in `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/shell/43_copilot_proxy.sh` (deployed to `~/.config/shell/43_copilot_proxy.sh`). Started detached via `nohup bunx "$pkg" start --port "$port"`. Managed with `copilot-proxy [start|stop|restart|status|logs|whoami|auth|shim]`.
  - PID file: `$TMPDIR/copilot-api-4141.pid`; log: `$TMPDIR/copilot-api-4141.log` (rotated ×3).

**Host:port / base_url:** `http://localhost:4141` (env `COPILOT_PROXY_PORT`, default `4141`).
```sh
_copilot_port() { printf '%s' "${COPILOT_PROXY_PORT:-4141}"; }
_copilot_base() { printf 'http://localhost:%s' "$(_copilot_port)"; }
```

**OpenAI-compatible?** Yes. It serves:
- `GET  /v1/models`
- `POST /v1/chat/completions` (OpenAI chat)
- `POST /v1/embeddings` (OpenAI embeddings — used by `copilot-embed`/`semsearch`)
- `POST /v1/messages` (native **Anthropic** Messages API passthrough — this is what Claude Code uses)
- `GET  /usage` (quota dashboard)

So a translation tool can hit it exactly like OpenAI: `POST http://localhost:4141/v1/chat/completions` with `{"model": "...", "messages": [...]}`.

**Auth:** No client-side API key. Downstream clients send a **dummy** bearer (`ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN=dummy`) — the proxy ignores it. The *real* credential is a GitHub `ghu_` device-login token minted by `copilot-proxy auth`, stored at `~/.local/share/copilot-api/github_token` (confirmed present, mode `0600`). The proxy exchanges it against `api.githubcopilot.com`. For OpenAI-compat calls from a script, no `Authorization` header is needed at all.

**Optional throttle shim:** A tiny Bun reverse-proxy (`dot_config/shell/copilot-throttle-shim.js`) can sit in front on port `4142` (`COPILOT_SHIM_PORT`) to cap concurrency and retry 403/429. Toggle: `copilot-proxy shim on`. When enabled, clients should target `http://localhost:4142`.

**Models exposed (verified live from `/v1/models` right now — proxy IS running):**
```
claude-opus-4-8  claude-opus-4-7  claude-opus-4-6  claude-opus-4-5
claude-sonnet-5  claude-sonnet-4-6  claude-sonnet-4-5  claude-haiku-4-5
gpt-5.5  gpt-5.4  gpt-5.4-mini  gpt-5.3-codex  gpt-5-mini
gemini-3.1-pro-preview  gemini-3.5-flash  gemini-3-flash-preview  gemini-2.5-pro
mai-code-1-flash-picker
text-embedding-3-small  text-embedding-3-small-inference  text-embedding-ada-002
```
opus-4-8 and sonnet-5 have **1M-token** context (`max_prompt_tokens: 936000`); haiku-4-5 is 200k. Embeddings are 1536-dim.

**Model-id gotcha (matters for a translation CLI):** Use **hyphenated** ids (`claude-opus-4-8`), never dotted (`claude-opus-4.8`) and never Anthropic's dated ids (`claude-opus-4-8-YYYYMMDD` → rejected `model_not_supported`). The `[1m]` suffix (`claude-opus-4-8[1m]`) is a **Claude-Code-only** hint that must be stripped before a raw API call — the proxy rejects a literal `...[1m]` model id.

**Key env vars (`43_copilot_proxy.sh`):**
| Env var | Default | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `COPILOT_PROXY_PORT` | `4141` | proxy listen port |
| `COPILOT_API_PKG` | `@jeffreycao/copilot-api@1.13.14` | bunx package spec |
| `COPILOT_PROXY_RATE` | `15` | `--rate-limit` (original pkg only) |
| `COPILOT_PROXY_QUIET` | `0` | inject Claude-Code quota-saver env |
| `COPILOT_PROXY_SHIM` / `COPILOT_SHIM_PORT` | off / `4142` | throttle shim |
| `COPILOT_CLAUDE_MODEL` | — | overrides pinned model; else `~/.local/state/copilot-proxy/model`; else `claude-opus-4-8[1m]` |

Full docs: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/docs/tools/copilot-claude-proxy.md` and `.../copilot-embeddings.md`. **Note the docs carry a Terms-of-Service warning** — using a Copilot sub to power non-GitHub agents violates Copilot ToS and can trigger abuse suspension. A translation tool hammering this endpoint carries the same risk.

## 2. LiteLLM

- **Installed** as a uv tool: `/Users/david/.local/bin/litellm` (confirmed on PATH). Install spec (`dot_ansible/roles/llm_tools/defaults/main.yml`): `uv tool install 'litellm[proxy]' --python 3.13 --with 'httpx[socks]'`.
- **No `config.yaml` and no proxy config exists anywhere in the repo.** There is no managed `litellm` invocation, no models list, no base_url configured. It is installed-but-unconfigured — only the bare CLI is present (`litellm --help`). If a translation tool wants a LiteLLM gateway it would have to be set up from scratch (default LiteLLM proxy port is `4000`, but nothing here uses it).

## 3. Ollama

- **Installed and working:** `/usr/local/bin/ollama`. Installed via Homebrew formula on macOS (see `llm_tools/tasks/main.yml`).
- **Models pulled (live `ollama list`):** **`llama3.2:3b`** (2.0 GB) — the only model currently present. Note: the shell default `AICAP_OLLAMA_MODEL` points at `qwen2.5-coder:7b`, but that model is **not** pulled — only `llama3.2:3b` is.
- **Default endpoint:** standard `http://localhost:11434`. No custom `OLLAMA_HOST`/`OLLAMA_MODELS` override anywhere in the dotfiles. OpenAI-compat path is `http://localhost:11434/v1/chat/completions` (explicitly documented as a supported `AICAP_HTTP_URL` target in `04_ai_capture.sh` and `05_ai_run.sh`).

## 4. API keys / secrets mechanism

Secrets are **NOT** stored in chezmoi, NOT templated, and there is **no 1Password/`op`, `pass`, or age-encryption** wiring for LLM keys. The mechanism is a set of **untracked user-local files** (listed in `.chezmoiignore.tmpl`, never overwritten). Documented in `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/docs/shells/adhoc-and-secrets.md`:

| File | Scope | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `~/.shellrc.secrets` | both shells (POSIX) | shared secrets (e.g. `export OPENAI_API_KEY=...`) — sourced by both `~/.zshrc` and `~/.bashrc` |
| `~/.config/zsh/secrets.zsh` | zsh only | zsh secrets |
| `~/.config/bash/secrets.sh` | bash only | bash secrets |
| `~/.shellrc.adhoc` / `~/.zshrc.adhoc` / `~/.bashrc.adhoc` | adhoc (non-secret) | overrides; also where `AICAP_*`/`COPILOT_*` env goes |

Load order: secrets are sourced **before** adhoc, shared before per-shell. The canonical example in the docs is literally `export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...` in `~/.shellrc.secrets`.

**Env var names the code actually reads:**
- `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` — used by the `http` agent as the bearer token (`AICAP_HTTP_API_KEY` overrides it). Reference: `04_ai_capture.sh:245` → `local key="${AICAP_HTTP_API_KEY:-${OPENROUTER_API_KEY:-}}"`.
- `ANTHROPIC_*` (`ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`, `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN`, `ANTHROPIC_MODEL`, `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_{OPUS,SONNET,HAIKU}_MODEL`, `ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL`) — set by the copilot-proxy layer to point Claude Code at localhost:4141 (auth token is the literal string `"dummy"`).
- No `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` consumer exists in the tracked code — those would only appear if the user placed them in the untracked secrets files (I did not and cannot read those). I found **no** evidence of a stored OpenAI or Anthropic first-party key in the repo.

**Important:** the actual secret files (`~/.shellrc.secrets`, etc.) are outside the repo and gitignored; I did not read their contents (they may not exist). The *mechanism* is plain `export` in an untracked sourced file — nothing encrypted.

## 5. Existing LLM CLI helpers

All are shell functions (POSIX sh / zsh), sourced from `~/.config/shell/`. Model/endpoint selection is driven by the **SSOT file** `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/shell/04_ai_agents.sh` (`AICAP_*` vars).

**Agent autodetect priority (`AICAP_AGENT_PRIORITY`):** `opencode claude codex agyc cursor-agent` — these are **coding-agent CLIs**, not raw API clients. Per-agent model pins: `AICAP_CLAUDE_MODEL=haiku`, `AICAP_OPENCODE_MODEL=github-copilot/claude-haiku-4.5`, `AICAP_OLLAMA_MODEL=qwen2.5-coder:7b`.

| Helper | File | What it does / how it picks model+endpoint |
|---|---|---|
| `aifix` / `aiexplain` (`aicapture`) | `04_ai_capture.sh` | capture terminal output → diagnose. Autodetects agent CLI; or `AICAP_AGENT=http` uses a raw **OpenAI-compatible curl** POST |
| `air` / `http` agent | `04_ai_capture.sh` | generic OpenAI-compat `curl` to `AICAP_HTTP_URL` (default `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions`, model `openrouter/free`; bearer from `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`). Reusable pattern: `payload=jq '{model,messages:[{role:user,content}],stream:false}'`, parse `.choices[0].message.content` |
| `ocr`/`ccr`/`cxr`/`cur`/`agr`/`olr` | `05_ai_run.sh` | one-shot prompt runners for opencode/claude/codex/cursor/antigravity/**ollama**. `olr "prompt"` → `ollama run "$AICAP_OLLAMA_MODEL"` |
| `aisuggest` | `dot_config/zsh/tools/05_aisuggest.zsh` | zsh prompt-time NL→shell ghost text; routes through the same agent layer (docs: `zsh-inline-ai.md`) |
| `copilot-embed` / `semsearch` | `44_copilot_embed.sh` + `scripts/semsearch.py` | embeddings + local semantic search via proxy `/v1/embeddings` (uses `AICAP_EMBED_MODEL=text-embedding-3-small`) |
| `claude-copilot` / `copilot-here` / `copilot-model` | `43_copilot_proxy.sh` | point Claude Code at the Copilot proxy |

There is **no** `simonw/llm`, `aichat`, `mods`, `aider`, or `sgpt` in this setup (checked — no references beyond incidental substring matches). The user's "generic HTTP LLM" path is deliberately the built-in `http` agent, not a third-party tool.

## 6. Preferred model / recommended default

- **Default model everywhere in the proxy layer is `claude-opus-4-8[1m]`** (the fallback in `_copilot_default_model`, and the injected `ANTHROPIC_MODEL`). Sonnet default is `claude-sonnet-5[1m]`, fast/small is `claude-haiku-4-5`. No global pin file is set (`~/.local/state/copilot-proxy/model` is absent), so the built-in `claude-opus-4-8[1m]` default is active.
- The user clearly has **first-class access to Claude Opus/Sonnet, GPT-5.x, and Gemini 3.x through the Copilot proxy** (verified live). Local Ollama has only `llama3.2:3b`.

## How to programmatically call each provider (for the translation CLI)

1. **Copilot proxy (recommended primary — running now, no key, best models):**
   - OpenAI style: `POST http://localhost:4141/v1/chat/completions`, body `{"model":"gpt-5.5","messages":[...]}`, **no auth header needed**.
   - Anthropic style: `POST http://localhost:4141/v1/messages`.
   - Discover models: `GET http://localhost:4141/v1/models`. Ensure up first (`copilot-proxy status`, or curl `/v1/models`); auto-start via `copilot-proxy start`. Use hyphenated ids, strip any `[1m]`.
   - Good default translation model: `claude-sonnet-5` (fast, cheap-ish, 1M ctx) or `gpt-5.4-mini` for volume; `claude-opus-4-8` for max quality. Caveat: Copilot ToS.

2. **Ollama (fully local, offline, no key):** `POST http://localhost:11434/v1/chat/completions` with `{"model":"llama3.2:3b",...}` (only that model is pulled), or native `ollama run`. Best for a private/offline default.

3. **OpenRouter (cloud fallback):** `POST https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions` with `Authorization: Bearer $OPENROUTER_API_KEY` (env may be set in `~/.shellrc.secrets`). Default model in dotfiles is `openrouter/free`.

4. **LiteLLM:** installed but unconfigured — no ready gateway to call. Would need a `config.yaml` + `litellm --config ...` (default port 4000) set up first.

**Suggested config approach for the new tool:** default `base_url=http://localhost:4141/v1` (OpenAI-compatible, no key), model `claude-sonnet-5`, with graceful fallback to Ollama `http://localhost:11434/v1` when the proxy isn't up. Read any key from the environment (`OPENROUTER_API_KEY` pattern) rather than a config file, matching this user's untracked-secrets convention.

---

# Translate CLI/TUI — how to make it feel native in this dotfiles environment

## Bottom line (the recipe)
A new `translate` tool should be a **Python single-file launcher using PEP 723 inline metadata run via `uv`**, using **tyro** (CLI) + **rich** (output) + **textual** (if TUI) + **platformdirs** (paths), placed at `dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_translate` with shared logic in a real package at `scripts/translate/`. Config in **`~/.config/translate/config.toml`** (TOML), history data in **`~/.local/share/translate/`** (sqlite), plus a **`tv translate` channel** for fuzzy history selection. This exactly mirrors the existing `yth` tool, which is the closest analog to "searchable translation history."

---

## 1. Existing custom in-house CLIs

Source lives in `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_*`, deployed by chezmoi to `~/.dotfiles/bin/` (mode 0755, first on PATH). Two languages only: **Python (PEP723/uv)** for anything nontrivial, **bash** for simple cross-platform utilities.

| CLI | Lang | Structure | CLI framework | TUI / interactive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **fleet** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Thin launcher + `scripts/fleet/*.py` (apply/tmux/info/pueue/exec/hosts) | **tyro** + rich + asyncssh | `fleet hosts` shells to `tv fleet-hosts` picker |
| **mlf** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Launcher + `scripts/mlf/*.py`, shared helpers in `scripts/mlf/__init__.py` | **tyro** + rich + plotext + **textual** | **Yes — bare `mlf` launches a Textual TUI dashboard**; `mlf tv` opens `tv mlflow` |
| **yth** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Launcher + `scripts/yth/*.py`, shared in `__init__.py` | **tyro** + rich + yt-dlp + platformdirs | **Bare `yth` execs `tv yth` picker**; sqlite FTS5 search |
| **pqsum** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Single 44k file | **argparse** (the outlier — not tyro) + rich | No |
| **mi-router** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Single file | **tyro** + httpx + rich + platformdirs | interactive getpass prompt |
| **x** | **bash** | single file | hand-rolled `usage()` | No (clipboard util, cross-platform fallbacks) |
| **ping-monitor** | **bash** | single file | hand-rolled | No |
| reyee / sms | Python (PEP723 uv) | single file | tyro | read-only router CLIs |

**The canonical launcher shebang + dependency block** (from `yth`, `mlf`, `fleet`, `pqsum`, `mi-router` — all identical form):
```python
#!/usr/bin/env -S uv run --quiet --script
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = [
#   "tyro>=0.9",
#   "rich>=13.9",
#   "platformdirs>=4",
# ]
# ///
```
No virtualenv, no install step — `uv` resolves deps on first run. **Python 3.11+** everywhere.

**Structure pattern (for anything with >1 subcommand):** thin dispatcher launcher in `dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_<name>` (holds the PEP723 deps + USAGE + argv routing) + a real Python package in `scripts/<name>/` with `__init__.py` holding shared helpers and one module per subcommand. The launcher discovers the scripts dir **at runtime via `chezmoi source-path`** (falls back to `~/.local/share/chezmoi`) so it works from any cwd. `mlf` and `yth` are the reference implementations; CLAUDE.md explicitly says "shared helpers live in `scripts/<tool>/__init__.py` — refactor there, not in the call sites."

---

## 2. XDG config conventions

Strongly XDG-first (docs: `docs/tools/xdg.md`, `docs/this_repo/config-conventions.md`). Rule: "prefer XDG to keep `$HOME` tidy."

- **Config → `~/.config/<tool>/config.toml`** — TOML is the preferred format. Read via `tomllib` and/or `platformdirs.user_config_dir`. Examples: `yth` uses `user_config_dir/yth/config.toml`; `mi-router` reads `~/.config/mi-router/config.toml`.
- **Data (irreplaceable) → `~/.local/share/<tool>/`** — e.g. `yth` sqlite DB at `platformdirs.user_data_dir/yth/history.db`.
- **Cache → `$XDG_CACHE_HOME/<tool>/`** — e.g. `pqsum` caches to `$XDG_CACHE_HOME/pqsum/<host>-<mode>.json`.
- **Config-resolution precedence** (idiomatic here, from `mi-router`): CLI flags → `~/.config/<tool>/config.toml` → env vars → interactive prompt (TTY only, offers to save).
- **Secrets:** never tracked; gitignored/chezmoi-ignored files sourced presence-gated, e.g. `~/.config/zsh/secrets.zsh`. For a translate API key: `~/.config/translate/config.toml` (chezmoi-ignored) or an env var pulled from the existing secrets file.

Format preference overall: **TOML for tool config**, YAML only for ansible, JSON reserved for machine-readable schemas/`--json` output.

---

## 3. How a new CLI gets on PATH & managed

**PATH mechanism:** `dot_config/shell/00_exports.sh.tmpl` line 26 prepends `~/.dotfiles/bin` first:
```
export PATH="$HOME/.dotfiles/bin:$HOME/bin:$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
```
So: drop `executable_translate` into `dot_dotfiles/bin/`, run `chezmoi apply`, and it's live from any cwd. **No `just` recipe or symlink needed** to register it (there are optional `just fleet`/`just mlf` passthrough recipes purely for dev convenience).

**If it should also be a standalone uv-tool binary** (not just a self-booting launcher), add an entry to `dot_ansible/roles/python_uv_tools/defaults/main.yml` (`- name: <pkg>` / `binary: <exe>`). This is the "dual pattern" CLAUDE.md describes for `mlf`↔`mlflow` and `yth`↔`yt-dlp`: deps declared in BOTH the launcher's PEP723 block (self-boot) AND `python_uv_tools` (standalone). For a from-scratch translate tool you likely only need the PEP723 launcher.

**CLAUDE.md cross-file "mirror" rules** triggered by adding a new `executable_*` CLI (`/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/CLAUDE.md`, rows 20–26):
- Tab completion in **two files** (kept in sync): `dot_config/zsh/tools/<NN>_translate_completion.zsh` + `dot_config/bash/<NN>_translate_completion.bash` (see `53_yth_completion.*` as the template; hand-written "Strategy B" like fleet/mlf/pqsum/x, or tyro-self-gen "Strategy A" like mi-router).
- `docs/tools/translate.md` reference page.
- Optional `tv` channel `dot_config/television/cable/translate.toml`.
- Update `docs/this_repo/tool-managers.md` A–Z index and `docs/shells/aliases.md` if it ships a completion helper.
- If surfaced to agents, mention in `dot_agents/skills/chezmoi-dotfiles/SKILL.md.tmpl` (re-renders each apply).

---

## 4. Television (tv) — ideal for translation-history fuzzy selection

~40+ custom channels in `dot_config/television/cable/*.toml`; matcher is **nucleo** (same as Helix). **`yth.toml` is a near-perfect template for a translation-history picker.** Its shape:
- `[source] command = "yth list --tsv"` emitting TSV; `display`/`output` use `{split:\t:N}` field extraction; `no_sort = true` because the list is already ordered temporally (the doc's rule #5: preserve order for temporal data).
- `[preview] command = "yth show '{...}' | bat ..."` (DB-only, instant).
- `[actions]` with `mode = "fork"`/`"execute"`; keybindings use **`Alt+`** letters because the tmux prefix is `Ctrl+b` (a `ctrl-b` binding never reaches tv).
- Bare `yth` (no args) execs `tv yth`.

**Important design constraint:** tv's source is single-shot and never sees the live query, so the channel can only fuzzy-match the display line (metadata). Deep/full-text search must be a **CLI subcommand** — that's why `yth search --subs` exists (sqlite FTS5) alongside the `tv yth` picker. A translate tool should do the same: `tv translate` picks over recent history by display line; `translate search <query>` does FTS over stored translations.

So the native pattern is: `translate list --tsv` → `tv translate` channel; bare `translate` execs `tv translate`; `translate search` for real text search.

---

## 5. fzf / gum usage (division of labor)

Doc: `docs/tools/tv-vs-fzf.md`. Both are used, with clear lanes:
- **fzf** owns shell-native integration: `Ctrl+R` (history/atuin), `Ctrl+T` (files), `Alt+C` (cd), `**<TAB>` completion. Config: `dot_config/zsh/tools/10_fzf.zsh`.
- **tv** owns structured domain pickers (sesh, tools, aliases, yth, mlflow) — declarative TOML channels. **This is the recommended path for a new domain picker like translation history.**
- **gum** is the idiom for interactive prompts *inside shell functions* (not fuzzy list-picking): e.g. `dot_config/shell/29_media.sh` `media-pick` uses `gum file` / `gum choose` / `gum input`; `dot_config/shell/55_macos_mem.sh` uses `gum confirm`.

For the translate tool: use **tv** for selecting from history; use **textual** (like `mlf`) if you want a full interactive TUI; use **gum** only if you add a quick shell-function wrapper needing a confirm/input prompt.

---

## 6. Existing translation tooling — NONE

A clean search across `dot_config`, `dot_dotfiles`, `dot_ansible`, and the aliases doc found **no `trans`, `translate`, DeepL, Google Translate, or translate-shell CLI/alias/function.** The only adjacent hits:
- A comment in `dot_config/homebrew/Brewfile.darwin.tmpl` mentioning the "Immersive Translate" **browser extension** (not a CLI).
- `dot_config/zsh/tools/05_aisuggest.zsh` — an AI ZLE widget (trigger `Alt+;`) that "translates" natural-language → a shell command (LLM-backed; unrelated to language translation, but worth knowing as prior art for an inline AI-powered widget).
- mkdocs i18n (`*.zh-TW.md`) doc translations — build tooling, not a translate CLI.

So this is greenfield; no naming/behavior collisions.

---

## 7. Config/secrets format + lint/format/test conventions

- **Config format:** TOML (via `tomllib`), stored under `~/.config/<tool>/`. Secrets: chezmoi-ignored file sourced presence-gated, or env var.
- **Python lint:** `.ruff_cache/` (ruff 0.15.20) exists at repo root, so **ruff is run ad-hoc** (editor/CLI, default rules) — but note it is **NOT wired into pre-commit and there is no `[tool.ruff]` config**; `pyproject.toml` only defines the mkdocs docs-build env, not these scripts. A root `pyrightconfig.json` exists for type-checking. `requires-python = ">=3.11"` is the standard.
- **Shell lint/format (enforced in `.pre-commit-config.yaml`):** shellcheck (`--severity=warning` for `scripts/*.sh`, `--severity=error` for `dot_config/{shell,bash}/`) + shfmt (`-i 2 -ci -bn -w`, i.e. 2-space indent). `.tmpl` and zsh files are excluded.
- **Other pre-commit hooks:** gitleaks + detect-private-key + a secret-redactor, standard whitespace/EOF/large-file/merge-conflict, check-toml/json/yaml, actionlint, and a prompt-generation sync check.
- **Tests:** `just test` = docker-test; bats tests exist; `just check` = `lint` + `chezmoi-dry-run`; `just check-all` = `lint bats docker-test`; `just lint` = ansible-syntax-check + pre-commit-run-all.

---

- Launcher templates: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_yth`, `.../executable_mlf`, `.../executable_fleet`
- Shared-package pattern: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/yth/` (`__init__.py` + per-subcommand modules), `scripts/mlf/`
- tv channel template: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/television/cable/yth.toml`
- Config conventions: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/docs/this_repo/config-conventions.md`, `docs/tools/xdg.md`
- PATH: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/shell/00_exports.sh.tmpl` (line 26)
- uv-tool declaration: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_ansible/roles/python_uv_tools/defaults/main.yml`
- Cross-file maintenance rules for new CLIs: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/CLAUDE.md` (rows 20–26)
- fzf vs tv guidance: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/docs/tools/tv-vs-fzf.md`
- Completion template: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/zsh/tools/53_yth_completion.zsh`

For history and memory, I'm using SQLite with FTS5 at `~/.local/share/translate/history.db` to track translations and enable search, plus a state file to remember the last language pair used. _**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-09 11:14:38Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="generic" data-tool-name="AskUserQuestion"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **AskUserQuestion**</summary>
**Which language/framework should the CLI+TUI use? Your existing custom CLIs (yth, mlf, fleet, mi-router) are all Python+uv+tyro+rich, and mlf already ships a Textual TUI — so Python maximizes consistency and reuse. The sub-choice is the interactive layer.**

Options:
- **Python + Textual** - Matches all your existing CLIs. Full interactive TUI (panes, history sidebar, live), rich CLI output, async. Most batteries-included; heaviest.
- **Python + prompt_toolkit** - Lighter REPL-style loop; snappiest type-and-translate feel, minimal chrome. Still Python+uv+tyro+rich for the CLI half.
- **Go + Bubble Tea** - Single static binary, very snappy, matches your Charm tooling (gum/glow/vhs/tv). But a new language for your custom-CLI set.
- **Rust + ratatui** - Fastest, single binary. Most implementation effort; immediate-mode UI. Least consistent with your Python CLI set.

**What should the DEFAULT translation engine be? (All can be configured/switched; this is just the out-of-box default.)**

Options:
- **Auto fallback chain** - Try copilot-proxy (best models) → Ollama (local) → free Google API. Most robust; always works even if proxy is down. Recommended.
- **copilot-proxy only** - claude-sonnet-5 via localhost:4141. Best quality, already running, no API key. Note: Copilot ToS discourages non-GitHub agents.
- **Ollama (local)** - llama3.2:3b, fully private/offline, no ToS concern. Weaker translation quality, only model currently pulled.
- **Free Google API** - Fast, no key, no LLM. Plain translation only — no grammar notes/alternatives/explanations.

**Which engine types should we actually BUILD in this first iteration? (You listed all three as in-scope; multi-select what's must-have for v1 vs. deferred.)** _(select multiple)_

Options:
- **LLM backend** - OpenAI-compatible client covering copilot-proxy, Ollama, OpenRouter, LiteLLM, generic base_url+key. Built-in prompts + structured output. The core.
- **Free translate API** - Google (+ optionally Bing) unofficial endpoints. No key, fast plain translation + language detection.
- **Dictionary lookup** - Word/phrase definitions via public dictionaries (dictionaryapi.dev / Wiktionary), exact match → fuzzy fallback.
- **History + memory** - sqlite-FTS history, tv picker, remembers last language pair + source auto-detect/fixed-target. (Recommended for v1.)

**How should the interactive TUI trigger inference? You said 'user typing stop, then infer ASAP' — which gesture do you mean?**

Options:
- **Enter = translate** - Enter submits immediately; Shift/Alt+Enter inserts a newline for multi-line input. Fast and conventional.
- **Live debounce** - Translates automatically ~400ms after you stop typing. Truest to 'when the user stops'. Costs more LLM calls.
- **Explicit submit key** - Type freely (multi-line), then Ctrl+Enter / Ctrl+D to run. Full control, never fires early.
- **Literal 'stop' sentinel** - Typing the word 'stop' on its own line triggers inference. Literal reading of your request.


**Answer:** Go + Bubble Tea" selected preview:
single binary `translate`
Bubble Tea + Lip Gloss
no uv/PEP723 launcher notes: + cobra, Auto fallback chain" selected preview:
copilot-proxy :4141
   ↓ (down?)
Ollama :11434
   ↓ (down?)
Google free API, LLM backend, Free translate API, Dictionary lookup, History + memory, Live debounce" selected preview:
type…(pause 400ms)→ auto-translate
edits re-trigger notes: + "Enter = translate" (live debounce as optional but default on)
</details></tool-use>

Now I'm thinking through the Go ecosystem choices—cobra for CLI, and for config I'm weighing viper versus a lighter approach with BurntSushi's TOML library. The tricky part is respecting XDG paths properly across platforms, since `os.UserConfigDir()` returns different defaults on macOS versus Linux, so I might need the adrg/xdg library to honor `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` consistently.

For the TUI stack, I'm using Bubble Tea with Lip Gloss for styling and Bubbles for components like textarea and textinput. For HTTP calls to OpenAI-compatible and Google endpoints, stdlib `net/http` works fine with SSE streaming for the LLM. For SQLite history, I'm leaning toward modernc.org/sqlite since it's pure Go with no CGO, making static binaries and cross-compilation easier, and it includes FTS5 support built-in.

For language detection offline, I'll use whatlanggo since it's lightweight, though the LLM path can detect via prompt or rely on backend detection. For fuzzy matching on language names and codes, I'll pick one of the standard Go fuzzy libraries like sahilm/fuzzy or agnivade/levenshtein.

The interactive TUI will use Bubble Tea's timer mechanism to debounce input—firing a translate command 400ms after the last keystroke, with Enter submitting immediately. I'll also cancel in-flight requests when the input changes using context cancellation to avoid showing stale results.

Now I'm sketching out the package structure with a root command that launches the TUI by default, and subcommands for one-off translation from arguments or stdin. - Config: XDG. ~/.config/translate/config.toml (TOML). Data (sqlite history) ~/.local/share/translate/. State (last pair) ~/.local/state/translate/. Auto-create on first run; `translate init` interactive wizard that PROBES which providers are up.
- Typo tolerance: cobra "did you mean" suggestions for subcommands; fuzzy-resolve language names/codes (e.g. "chinees"->zh); dictionary exact->fuzzy.
- copilot-proxy live facts: OpenAI-compat at :4141, GET /v1/models lists claude-opus-4-8, claude-sonnet-5, claude-haiku-4-5, gpt-5.5, gpt-5.4-mini, gemini-3.x. MODEL-ID GOTCHA: use hyphenated ids, strip any "[1m]" suffix, never dated ids. Copilot ToS discourages non-GitHub agents (note as caveat). Recommend default model claude-sonnet-5; fast=claude-haiku-4-5 or gpt-5.4-mini; max=claude-opus-4-8; offline=llama3.2:3b (only Ollama model pulled).
- "Marvin-lite" aspiration: model a translation task as a typed struct TranslateResult{ Translation, DetectedSource, Target, Alternatives[], Notes, Confidence } and get it back as structured JSON from the LLM (built-in prompt + JSON schema / response_format), so results are richer than plain text. Full generic function->LLM runtime is OUT OF SCOPE for v1 but design should not preclude it.
- Built-in prompts required (translate; typo-tolerant "interpret intended meaning"; concise faithful register-aware). Grammar-correction, vocab storage, spaced review are FUTURE, not v1.


VERIFY & RECOMMEND:
1. Confirm current import paths + roles: charmbracelet/bubbletea, charmbracelet/lipgloss, charmbracelet/bubbles (which components exist: textarea, textinput, viewport, list, spinner, help, key), charmbracelet/huh (interactive forms for the init wizard: Select, Input, Confirm, MultiSelect; can huh run standalone AND embedded in a Bubble Tea model?).
2. The DEBOUNCE + CANCEL + STREAM pattern in Bubble Tea (Elm architecture): how to (a) start a ~400ms debounce timer after each keystroke and fire an auto-translate only if idle — is tea.Tick with a generation/seq counter the right approach? (b) cancel an in-flight translation when input changes again (context.Context stored in model, cancel on new input), (c) stream LLM tokens into the result pane live (goroutine reading an SSE/channel, emitting tea.Msg per chunk via a self-resubscribing tea.Cmd). Give the canonical message-passing shapes (custom msg structs, tea.Cmd returning tea.Msg, how to avoid rendering stale/cancelled results using a request id). Keep it concrete.
3. Layout: recommended lipgloss approach for a two-pane layout (input+result on left, history list on right) with a status/footer line, responsive to WindowSizeMsg.
4. Any gotcha: does reading stdin-pipe input conflict with Bubble Tea taking over the TTY (i.e. detect `!isatty` to choose one-shot vs TUI)?

<summary>Tool use: **WebSearch**</summary>
 `pelletier/go-toml v2 vs BurntSushi/toml 2025 arrays of tables idiomatic`

```text
Web search results for query: "pelletier/go-toml v2 vs BurntSushi/toml 2025 arrays of tables idiomatic"

Links: [{"title":"toml package - github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2 - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2?utm_source=openai"},{"title":"GitHub - pelletier/go-toml: Go library for the TOML file format · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/pelletier/go-toml"},{"title":"GitHub - BurntSushi/toml: TOML parser for Golang with reflection. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/BurntSushi/toml"},{"title":"raw.githubusercontent.com","url":"https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BurntSushi/toml/master/encode.go"}]

I ran the web search you asked for and pulled together the key findings about how pelletier/go-toml (v2) and BurntSushi/toml handle TOML "arrays of tables" (the [[table]] syntax) and what is idiomatic when using those Go libraries.

Summary (short):

- pelletier/go-toml (v2)
  - Actively documents two-way encoding/decoding and explicitly encodes a top-level slice-of-maps-or-structs as an array-of-tables (i.e. it will emit a top-level [[table]] sequence when you Marshal/Encode a top-level []struct or []map). It also exposes encoder options (struct tags, multiline arrays, SetArraysMultiline, inline, omitempty, etc.). ([pkg.go.dev](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2?utm_source=openai))
  - The project targets TOML v1.1.0 and is presented as a modern, full-featured encoder+decoder for programmatic TOML use. ([github.com](https://github.com/pelletier/go-toml))

- BurntSushi/toml
  - Supports decoding arrays of tables into a Go slice (i.e. [[servers]] → []Server), and is a widely used, stable TOML parser for Go. ([github.com](https://github.com/BurntSushi/toml))
  - Its encoder, however, treats top-level arrays-of-tables specially: the encoder implementation will error if asked to emit an array-of-tables with no key (i.e. it rejects encoding a bare top-level slice as [[...]] because a key is required). In short: BurntSushi can decode arrays-of-tables; encoding a top-level slice into [[table]] is not allowed by the encoder (it expects top-level maps/structs or arrays-of-tables under keys). See the encoder source (errNoKey / top-level values must be Go maps or structs). ([raw.githubusercontent.com](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BurntSushi/toml/master/encode.go))

Idiomatic guidance (practical):
- If you need two-way TOML (programmatically read, modify, and write TOML) and want to emit top-level arrays-of-tables (or prefer more encoder options), pelletier/go-toml v2 is the more convenient/idiomatic choice — it was designed to encode top-level []struct -> [[table]] and provides encoder controls and struct tag options. ([pkg.go.dev](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2?utm_source=openai))
- If your use-case is primarily parsing/decoding TOML into Go structs (reading configs) and you want a stable, battle-tested decoder, BurntSushi/toml remains a solid choice — but be aware of the encoder limitations around top-level array-of-tables. For decoding arrays-of-tables to []struct, BurntSushi is idiomatic and common. ([github.com](https://github.com/BurntSushi/toml))

If you want, I can:
- Show minimal Go code examples demonstrating (a) pelletier encoding a top-level []struct to [[table]] and (b) BurntSushi decoding a [[table]] into []struct (and show what fails when you try to Marshal a top-level slice with BurntSushi).  
- Search for more 2025-era community discussion / issue threads comparing the two specifically on arrays-of-tables (performance, edge cases, spec compatibility), and surface any notable gotchas.

Which follow-up would you like?


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.
```
</details></tool-use>
