<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://github.com/charmbracelet/huh`

```text
- Current import path: "charm.land/huh/v2"  
- Current latest release shown: "v2.0.3"

Yes — forms can be run standalone with Run(). Also, huh forms can be embedded in Bubble Tea; as the docs state, "huh.Form is just a tea.Model".

Field types available (from the page): Select, Input, Confirm, MultiSelect, Text.

Minimal examples (paraphrased):

Standalone (blocking form.Run()):
package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "log"

    "charm.land/huh/v2"
)

func main() {
    var name string
    f := huh.NewForm(
        huh.NewGroup(
            huh.NewInput().
                Title("Your name").
                Value(&name),
        ),
    )

    if err := f.Run(); err != nil {
        log.Fatal(err)
    }
    fmt.Println("Hello,", name)
}

Embedded in Bubble Tea (form used as a tea.Model):
package main

import (
    "fmt"

    tea "github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea"
    "charm.land/huh/v2"
)

type Model struct {
    form *huh.Form
}

func NewModel() Model {
    var role string
    frm := huh.NewForm(
        huh.NewGroup(
            huh.NewSelect[string]().
                Key("role").
                Options(huh.NewOption("Dev", "dev"), huh.NewOption("Ops", "ops")).
                Title("Pick role").
                Value(&role),
        ),
    )
    return Model{form: frm}
}

func (m Model) Init() tea.Cmd { return m.form.Init() }

func (m Model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    f, cmd := m.form.Update(msg)
    if fm, ok := f.(*huh.Form); ok { m.form = fm }
    return m, cmd
}

func (m Model) View() string {
    if m.form.State == huh.StateCompleted {
        return fmt.Sprintf("Done: %s", m.form.GetString("role"))
    }
    return m.form.View()
}

Notes:
- I quoted the exact import path, release tag, and the short doc phrase "huh.Form is just a tea.Model".  
- The rest is paraphrased or original sample code illustrating standalone Run() and Bubble Tea embedding.
```
</details></tool-use>

Links: [{"title":"SQLite FTS5 Extension","url":"https://www.sqlite.org/fts5.html?utm_source=openai"},{"title":"fts package - gosqlite.org/fts - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/gosqlite.org%40v0.10.0/fts"},{"title":"C# SQLite FTS5 Table and Triger creation - Stack Overflow","url":"https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51601208/c-sharp-sqlite-fts5-table-and-triger-creation?utm_source=openai"},{"title":"SQLite User Forum: Crash in FTS5 Module","url":"https://sqlite.org/forum/forumpost/81670d1056?t=c&utm_source=openai"},{"title":"SQLite User Forum: Windows x64 SQLite extensions...","url":"https://www.sqlite.org/forum/info/6c5eea3698a45c96?utm_source=openai"},{"title":"SQLite User Forum: ’SQL logic error no such module: fts5’","url":"https://sqlite.org/forum/info/4cf36abaf7011e76cbb73e07591d4a0cae6bcc9b7c2fe5e548f6fed02710e503?utm_source=openai"},{"title":"sqlite package - modernc.org/sqlite - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite"}]

I ran that search. Short summary of what I found plus recommended checks:

Findings
- The SQLite FTS5 module is a compile-time feature: it must either be built into the SQLite library (SQLITE_ENABLE_FTS5 / --enable-fts5) or provided as a loadable extension. If it is not present you will get errors like "no such module: fts5" when running CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE ... USING fts5. ([sqlite.org](https://www.sqlite.org/fts5.html?utm_source=openai))
- Several Go packages and docs that layer on top of modernc.org/sqlite state that FTS5 is compiled into the modernc/ czNic SQLite build by default (i.e., no separate extension to load). Example: the gosqlite fts wrapper explicitly says “FTS5 is compiled into modernc.org/sqlite/lib … by default — no extension to load.” ([pkg.go.dev](https://pkg.go.dev/gosqlite.org%40v0.10.0/fts))
- However, whether FTS5 is available depends on the specific SQLite build used by your runtime / OS / package — many distributions/embeddings historically compiled SQLite without FTS5, so CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE USING fts5 can fail unless that build flag is present. There are many reports (StackOverflow / forums) of the “no such module: fts5” error for builds without the feature. ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51601208/c-sharp-sqlite-fts5-table-and-triger-creation?utm_source=openai))
- There have also been FTS5-related bugs reported in 2024 (e.g., tokenizer-argument parsing crashes) that were fixed in SQLite trunk / released versions (fixes shipped around SQLite 3.46.1). If you saw a crash in mid‑2024 it may be related to that bug. ([sqlite.org](https://sqlite.org/forum/forumpost/81670d1056?t=c&utm_source=openai))

How to verify on your system (quick checks)
- Query compile options at runtime:
  - PRAGMA compile_options;  -- look for ENABLE_FTS5 in the list.  
  - Or: SELECT sqlite_compileoption_used('ENABLE_FTS5');  -- returns 1 if the library was built with that option. (These are supported ways to detect compile-time options.) ([sqlite.org](https://www.sqlite.org/forum/info/6c5eea3698a45c96?utm_source=openai))
- Or simply try to create an FTS5 virtual table and catch the error:
  - CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE test_fts USING fts5(content);
  - If the connection returns "no such module: fts5", the running SQLite library lacks FTS5. (This is the common symptom referenced in many reports.) ([sqlite.org](https://sqlite.org/forum/info/4cf36abaf7011e76cbb73e07591d4a0cae6bcc9b7c2fe5e548f6fed02710e503?utm_source=openai))

Relevant sources I used
- modernc/ czNic package docs (modernc.org/sqlite / pkg.go.dev) — driver details, virtual-table API and shipped SQLite versions. ([pkg.go.dev](https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite))  
- gosqlite.org fts package (explicit note that FTS5 is compiled into modernc.org/sqlite/lib by default). ([pkg.go.dev](https://pkg.go.dev/gosqlite.org%40v0.10.0/fts))  
- SQLite FTS5 docs and build/enable instructions (SQLITE_ENABLE_FTS5 / --enable-fts5). ([sqlite.org](https://www.sqlite.org/fts5.html?utm_source=openai))  
- Forum/StackOverflow examples showing “no such module: fts5” when FTS5 is not available. ([sqlite.org](https://sqlite.org/forum/info/4cf36abaf7011e76cbb73e07591d4a0cae6bcc9b7c2fe5e548f6fed02710e503?utm_source=openai))  
- SQLite forum report and fix note for an FTS5 tokenizer crash reported in 2024 (fixed in trunk / 3.46.1). ([sqlite.org](https://sqlite.org/forum/forumpost/81670d1056?t=c&utm_source=openai))

If you want, I can:
- Check a specific modernc.org/sqlite release (give me the version tag or a go.mod line) to confirm whether that release was built with FTS5; or
- Show the exact SQL commands to run to detect/diagnose FTS5 availability in your environment and interpret results.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://github.com/adrg/xdg`

```text
Short answer
- On macOS the library defaults XDG_CONFIG_HOME, XDG_DATA_HOME and XDG_STATE_HOME to "~/Library/Application Support" when the env vars are not set.
- It does honor environment variables (they "take precedence"); if XDG_CONFIG_HOME etc. are set, those values are used instead of the macOS defaults.

Quoted evidence (≤125 chars)
- "On macOS ... ~/Library/Application Support"  
- "XDG user directories environment variables are usually not set on most operating systems. However, if they are present in the environment, they take precedence."

API names and package vars
- Functions you asked for:
  - ConfigFile(name string) (string, error)
  - DataFile(name string) (string, error)
  - StateFile(name string) (string, error)
  - SearchConfigFile(name string) (string, error)
  - (also SearchDataFile, SearchStateFile, CacheFile, RuntimeFile etc. mentioned in docs)
- Package-level variables:
  - xdg.ConfigHome
  - xdg.DataHome
  - xdg.StateHome
  - xdg.CacheHome
  - xdg.RuntimeDir
  - xdg.BinHome
  - xdg.Home
  - xdg.ApplicationDirs
  - xdg.FontDirs
  - xdg.DataDirs
  - xdg.ConfigDirs
  - xdg.UserDirs (struct with Desktop, Download, Documents, Music, Pictures, Videos, Templates, PublicShare, Projects)

Import path
- github.com/adrg/xdg

Minimal Go usage sketch
- import "github.com/adrg/xdg"
- Access defaults: xdg.ConfigHome, xdg.DataHome, xdg.StateHome
- Create/find files:
  - cfg, err := xdg.ConfigFile("appname/config.yaml")
  - df, err := xdg.DataFile("appname/data.db")
  - sf, err := xdg.StateFile("appname/state.json")
  - found, err := xdg.SearchConfigFile("appname/config.yaml")

Notes
- On macOS the package uses the macOS-specific default locations (Library/Application Support and Caches) rather than ~/.config by default unless the XDG_* env vars are explicitly set.
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
Summary of the upgrade guide (concise):

Exact new import path
- The guide uses: "charm.land/bubbletea/v2" (not github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/v2).

Major breaking changes (based on the guide)
- Init signature
  - Init still returns a tea.Cmd in examples (e.g., func (m model) Init() tea.Cmd { return nil }).
- Update return types
  - Update still returns (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) in examples.
- View signature
  - View no longer returns string; it now returns a tea.View: "View() tea.View".
- Key handling
  - KeyMsg is now an interface; use tea.KeyPressMsg for key presses.
  - Fields changed: msg.Type → msg.Code; msg.Runes → msg.Text (string); msg.Alt → msg.Mod.
  - Space string changed: use "space" when matching msg.String().
  - Ctrl+C: no tea.KeyCtrlC constant — match "ctrl+c" or check Code+Mod.
- Cursor
  - Imperative cursor commands removed; control cursor via the View's Cursor field (e.g., view.Cursor = nil to hide, or view.Cursor = &tea.Cursor{...} / tea.NewCursor).
- WindowSize / RequestWindowSize
  - tea.WindowSize() renamed to tea.RequestWindowSize; it "now returns Msg directly, not a Cmd".

Is v2 released / stable?
- The guide links the v2 release tag ("What's New") and shows examples for v2 (including v2.0.0). The document presents v2 as a released upgrade (v2.0.0).

Selected short quotes from the guide (<=125 chars)
- "View() tea.View"
- "charm.land/bubbletea/v2"
- "now returns Msg directly, not a Cmd"

([github.com](https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/blob/main/examples/debounce/main.go))

- charmbracelet/bubbletea — examples index / repo — the repository examples (including the debounce example) and other command/timer examples. Useful for other command orchestration patterns. ([github.com](https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/tree/main/examples))

- pkg.go.dev / charm.land docs for tea.Tick — documentation describing tea.Tick (how to produce a command that waits a duration and returns a message) and the common beginner note that Tick produces a single message (use tea.Every or re-return Tick for repeating behavior). This explains why debounce commonly uses a single Tick per keystroke plus a tag check. ([pkg.go.dev](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea))

- DeepWiki / blog posts on commands and orchestration — explanations of Bubble Tea’s command system and examples (Every vs Tick, command orchestration patterns). Good background reading for why the counter+Tick debouncing approach is used. ([deepwiki.com](https://deepwiki.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/6.4-command-orchestration-examples))

- bubbletea-rs (Rust) debounce example / docs — a debounce example/implementation in the Rust port and docs mentioning timer cancellation semantics (use cancel_timer when appropriate). ```text
package main

// This example illustrates how to debounce commands.
//
// When the user presses a key we increment the "tag" value on the model and,
// after a short delay, we include that tag value in the message produced
// by the Tick command.
//
// In a subsequent Update, if the tag in the Msg matches current tag on the
// model's state we know that the debouncing is complete and we can proceed as
// normal. If not, we simply ignore the inbound message.

import (
	"fmt"
	"os"
	"time"

	tea "charm.land/bubbletea/v2"
)

const debounceDuration = time.Second

type exitMsg int

type model struct {
	tag int
}

func (m model) Init() tea.Cmd {
	return nil
}

func (m model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
	switch msg := msg.(type) {
	case tea.KeyPressMsg:
		// Increment the tag on the model...
		m.tag++
		return m, tea.Tick(debounceDuration, func(_ time.Time) tea.Msg {
			// ...and include a copy of that tag value in the message.
			return exitMsg(m.tag)
		})
	case exitMsg:
		// If the tag in the message doesn't match the tag on the model then we
		// know that this message was not the last one sent and another is on
		// the way. If that's the case we know, we can ignore this message.
		// Otherwise, the debounce timeout has passed and this message is a
		// valid debounced one.
		if int(msg) == m.tag {
			return m, tea.Quit
		}
	}

	return m, nil
}

func (m model) View() tea.View {
	return tea.NewView(fmt.Sprintf("Key presses: %d", m.tag) +
		"\nTo exit press any key, then wait for one second without pressing anything.")
}

func main() {
	if _, err := tea.NewProgram(model{}).Run(); err != nil {
		fmt.Println("uh oh:", err)
		os.Exit(1)
	}
}
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
Summary of the top-level JSON-array layout (skeleton with placeholders):

[
  0: [ ... ],              // index 0 — main translation pairs (source/target tokens)
  1: { ... },              // index 1 — the dictionary / "bd" block: parts of speech + senses
  2: ... ,                 // index 2 — target language code or misc marker (e.g. "en")
  3: ... ,                 // index 3 — (unused here / null)
  4: ... ,                 // index 4 — (unused here / null)
  5: [ ... ],              // index 5 — alternate translations / examples list
  ...,
  last: [ ... ]            // last index — metadata / empty list in this sample
]

What each requested index contains (concise):

- Index 0:
  - Holds the core token mappings: arrays of source words with their top translation(s).
  - Example short excerpt: "['livre','book',null,null,10]" (quote <=125 chars).

- Index 1 (dictionary / "bd" block):
  - A structured array of parts-of-speech entries. Each POS entry contains:
    - POS label (e.g. "verb" or "noun")
    - List of senses/groups: each sense has the headword, synset translations, optional sense metadata (frequency, gender, etc.)
    - The canonical target lemma string and POS-level metadata.
  - Example tiny fragment: "['verb', ['réserver', ...], 'book', 2]" (quote <=125 chars).

- Index 2:
  - A short scalar, here the target language code ("en") or a related marker.

- Index 5 (alternate translations):
  - An array of alternate candidate translations with context lists and scoring/occurrence info.
  - In the sample it contains entries like:
    - headword, null, [ [ lemma, null, bool,bool,[score] ], ... ], additional arrays
  - Example fragment: "['book',null,[[...]],[[0,4]],'book',0,0]" (quote <=125 chars).

- Last index:
  - In this file the final element is an empty list (metadata/auxiliary data placeholder).
  - Shown as: "[]" (quote <=125 chars).

Notes:
- The dictionary block at index 1 is where POS-level groups and per-sense term lists live.
- Indexes between 2 and 5 may be null or hold auxiliary markers; alternate translations are explicitly grouped at index 5.
```

I searched the web for "lingua-go vs whatlanggo language detection accuracy short text Go library" and pulled the most relevant pages and benchmarks. Below are the top results with short notes and sources you can follow up on.

Top search hits / quick summary
- lingua-go (repo + description): "The most accurate natural language detection library for Go, suitable for short text and mixed-language text." (official GitHub repo). ([github.com](https://github.com/pemistahl/lingua-go))  
- whatlanggo (repo): official whatlanggo GitHub (pure-Go language detector). ([github.com](https://github.com/abadojack/whatlanggo))  
- Benchmark repo (punitsu): an independent benchmark comparing Whatlang vs Lingua with reported accuracy & time figures (shows Lingua higher accuracy in their tests; Whatlang faster). Contains the benchmark code. ([github.com](https://github.com/punitsu/benchmark-whatlang-vs-lingua))  
- pkg.go.dev page for lingua: notes that Lingua "yields pretty accurate results on both long and short text, even on single words and phrases." ([pkg.go.dev](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pemistahl/lingua-go))

What the results say (short interpretation)
- Multiple sources and an independent benchmark show Lingua tends to prioritize accuracy on short or noisy text (claims and tests indicate stronger short-text accuracy). ([pkg.go.dev](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pemistahl/lingua-go))  
- Whatlanggo is a lighter-weight, older pure-Go detector that is commonly used for speed/compactness; benchmarks typically show it is faster but less accurate than Lingua on the datasets tested. ([github.com](https://github.com/abadojack/whatlanggo))  
- Benchmarks and accuracy depend a lot on the dataset, number of target languages, and minimum text length — results can vary substantially by those factors. ```text
Short answer
- The page doesn’t explicitly mention “FTS5” or document full-text search support.
- The bundled SQLite amalgamation used by this driver is shown as SQLite 3.53.2 on supported platforms, and that upstream SQLite 3.53.2 ships with FTS5 in the core distribution — so FTS5 is available in the upstream SQLite version used by this module. However, the docs on this page do not state “FTS5 is compiled in by default” or call out any FTS5-specific build flags or limitations.

What I can reliably extract from the page
- Import path: modernc.org/sqlite
- Driver name (for database/sql): "sqlite"
  - Example usage from the docs:
    import (
        "database/sql"
        _ "modernc.org/sqlite"
    )
    db, err := sql.Open("sqlite", dsnURI)
- SQLite version used by the module (per “Supported platforms and architectures”): 3.53.2 (per-platform rows shown on the page).
- There is no explicit documentation text on this page that says “FTS5 is enabled” or “FTS5 is disabled” or that gives a version/commit where FTS5 support was added.

How to confirm / what to do next
- If you need a firm guarantee that FTS5 is present and enabled in the exact module version you use:
  1. Inspect the repository (lib/ or build files) for references to FTS5 (grep for "FTS5", "fts5", or sqlite3_enable_fts5).
  2. Build and query sqlite for FTS5 availability at runtime:
     - Create a connection and run: SELECT sqlite_version(), sqlite_compileoption_used('FTS5');
     - If sqlite_compileoption_used('FTS5') returns 1 (or the compile options list contains FTS5), it was compiled in.
  Example:
      db, _ := sql.Open("sqlite", ":memory:")
      var v, fts5 string
      _ = db.QueryRow("select sqlite_version()").Scan(&v)
      _ = db.QueryRow("select sqlite_compileoption_used('FTS5')").Scan(&fts5)
      // fts5 == "1" => compiled in
- Alternatively, check the module’s lib/ generated C source or build scripts in the repo (modernc.org/sqlite on GitLab) for explicit enable/disable build flags or references to FTS5.

Notes / caveats
- The module page warns about a fragile dependency on modernc.org/libc (you should use the same modernc.org/libc version as in the repo’s go.mod) — if you build a custom debug/development version be mindful of matching libc versions.
- Because the module ships a transpiled SQLite C amalgamation (see lib/), whether an extension or compile option is present is determined by how that amalgamation was generated/compiled into the Go sources — the page itself does not assert the FTS5 state.

If you want, I can:
- Search the repository source (lib/ or build files) for "FTS5" or compile options and report back.
- Show the exact code to run the sqlite_compileoption_used check and interpret results.
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/main/examples/realtime/main.go`

```text
Below is a concise explanation of the pattern shown in the provided example (paraphrased; selected short quoted lines are kept under 125 characters).

Summary of the runtime pattern
- A goroutine continuously sends empty notifications on a channel (simulating external activity).
- A tea.Cmd blocks waiting for the next value from that channel and, when it receives one, returns a message to Bubble Tea.
- The model's Update handler processes that message and re-issues the same waiting Cmd so the loop continues (self‑resubscribing).

Key pieces (short exact excerpts quoted)

- goroutine generating events:
  "sub <- struct{}{}"

- Cmd that waits for channel activity and returns a message:
  "return responseMsg(<-sub)"

- Update that handles the message and re-schedules the waiting Cmd:
  "case responseMsg:
    m.responses++
    return m, waitForActivity(m.sub)"

How they interact (in plain language)
- The program creates a channel (make(chan struct{})) and starts a goroutine that writes to it at irregular intervals.
- A Cmd (waitForActivity) executes, blocks on reading from that channel, and when a value arrives returns a responseMsg into the Bubble Tea event loop.
- In Update, receiving responseMsg causes the model to update state (increment a counter) and then return waitForActivity(m.sub) as the next Cmd, so the Cmd will again wait on the channel for the next event.
- Meanwhile, a separate listenForActivity goroutine keeps pushing events into the channel independent of the Cmd; the Cmd consumes them one at a time and re-schedules itself to continue consuming future events.

This demonstrates the pattern: goroutine -> channel -> blocking Cmd -> message -> Update re-schedules Cmd, yielding a continuous, real-time event flow.
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://github.com/charmbracelet/lipgloss`

```text
Short answer — v2 keeps the same high‑level layout APIs but colors and runtime color selection changed.

What stayed (APIs you asked about)
- lipgloss.NewStyle() — still present and used to build styles.
- JoinHorizontal / JoinVertical — still available for composing blocks.
- Place — still available (PlaceHorizontal / PlaceVertical / Place).
- Width / Height (and SetString) — still available on Style.
- Border / BorderStyle / BorderForeground etc. — still supported (including helpers like MarkdownBorder/ASCIIBorder).

How colors work in v2 (summary from the docs)
- lipgloss.Color supports ANSI‑16, ANSI‑256 and TrueColor (hex) and named ANSI constants. Example: lipgloss.Color("#0000FF"), lipgloss.Color("86").
- Lip Gloss "automatically downsampling colors to the best available profile" — use the lipgloss writer helpers (lipgloss.Println / Sprint / Fprint ...) when not used inside Bubble Tea so colors are downsampled when output is not a TTY.
- Adaptive and profile‑complete APIs:
  - Use LightDark / HasDarkBackground to pick light vs dark variants at runtime (HasDarkBackground(os.Stdin, os.Stdout)).
  - For exact per‑profile colors, use Complete(profile) with colorprofile.Detect and then call the returned function with ansi, ansi256, truecolor variants.
- The compat package provides migration helpers (AdaptiveColor, CompleteColor, CompleteAdaptiveColor) but "we don't recommend this for new code as it removes the purity from Lip Gloss" (compat does I/O globally).

How to set foreground / background in v2
- Use style methods: Foreground(lipgloss.Color(...)) and Background(lipgloss.Color(...)). Example shown: NewStyle().Foreground(lipgloss.Color("229")).Background(lipgloss.Color("63")).

How to detect terminal background color in v2
- In Bubble Tea: request the background and handle tea.BackgroundColorMsg (example sends tea.RequestBackgroundColor and uses msg.IsDark()).
- Standalone: call HasDarkBackground(os.Stdin, os.Stderr) (or os.Stdout in examples) and then use LightDark(hasDarkBG) to choose colors.

Relevant short quotes (<=125 chars)
- "automatically downsampling colors to the best available profile."
- "HasDarkBackground(os.Stdin, os.Stdout)"
- "Foreground(lipgloss.Color(\"#f1f1f1\"))" (example of setting foreground)
- "we don't recommend this for new code as it removes the purity from Lip Gloss"

If you want, I can extract the exact example snippets (Style creation, LightDark usage or Bubble Tea example) and keep each quoted line within 125 chars.
```
</details></tool-use>

Read This Before You Start | Yorukot","url":"https://yorukot.me/blog/before-you-build-a-tui-or-cli-app/?utm_source=openai"}]

Done — I searched for resources that match your query (detecting whether stdin is a TTY/pipe in Go, IsTerminal / os.Stdin.Fd(), and guidance about choosing an interactive TUI vs a one‑shot CLI with Cobra). Key findings and authoritative links:

Quick summary (most useful items)
- Use golang.org/x/term.IsTerminal(int(os.Stdin.Fd())) to test whether a file descriptor is a terminal. This is the modern, standard approach. ([golang.org](https://golang.org/x/term?utm_source=openai))  
- Common alternative / small packages: github.com/mattn/go-isatty (IsTerminal / IsCygwinTerminal helpers). ([chromium.googlesource.com](https://chromium.googlesource.com/external/github.com/mattn/go-isatty.git/%2B/40c9792ed649bfe7121aec8bf74074b0008fece9/README.md?utm_source=openai))  
- Another simple method is checking os.Stdin.Stat() and testing the os.ModeCharDevice bit — if (stat.Mode() & os.ModeCharDevice) == 0 then stdin is coming from a pipe/file (not a terminal). This is widely used in examples/answers. ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22744443/check-if-there-is-something-to-read-on-stdin-in-golang?utm_source=openai))  
- Cobra (spf13/cobra) itself doesn’t automatically choose between TUI and one‑shot modes; you detect interactivity (isatty) in your command and branch to either a non‑interactive workflow or launch your TUI (e.g., bubbletea/tview/etc.). See Cobra repo for the CLI framework and examples. ([github.com](https://github.com/spf13/cobra?utm_source=openai))  
- Design best practices: prefer explicit flags (--interactive / --no-interactive, --output=json), and follow CLI principles (strip ANSI when stdout not a TTY, honor non-interactive behavior). See CLI Spec and various blog posts on when to build a TUI vs a simple CLI. ([clispec.dev](https://clispec.dev/?utm_source=openai))

Representative links (from the search)
- golang.org/x/term (IsTerminal) — package docs. ([golang.org](https://golang.org/x/term?utm_source=openai))  
- Stack Overflow: os.Stdin.Stat() + os.ModeCharDevice examples and discussion. ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22744443/check-if-there-is-something-to-read-on-stdin-in-golang?utm_source=openai))  
- github.com/mattn/go-isatty (usage/examples). ([chromium.googlesource.com](https://chromium.googlesource.com/external/github.com/mattn/go-isatty.git/%2B/40c9792ed649bfe7121aec8bf74074b0008fece9/README.md?utm_source=openai))  
- spf13/cobra (GitHub repo). ([github.com](https://github.com/spf13/cobra?utm_source=openai))  
- Articles / best-practice posts about choosing TUI vs CLI and design guidance (blog post on TUI vs CLI, CLI Spec). ([yorukot.me](https://yorukot.me/blog/before-you-build-a-tui-or-cli-app/?utm_source=openai))

Small example snippets (from those sources)
- Using x/term:
  term.IsTerminal(int(os.Stdin.Fd()))
  (x/term docs show IsTerminal taking an int FD). ([golang.org](https://golang.org/x/term?utm_source=openai))
- Using os.Stdin.Stat():
  stat, _ := os.Stdin.Stat()
  if (stat.Mode() & os.ModeCharDevice) == 0 {
      // piped or file
  } else {
      // terminal
  }
  (common StackOverflow pattern). ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22744443/check-if-there-is-something-to-read-on-stdin-in-golang?utm_source=openai))

Practical recommendations for Cobra-based tools
- Detect interactivity early (IsTerminal or Stat()) and branch: if piped → non-interactive/one-shot; if terminal → allow interactive prompts or start TUI. Cite: Cobra + isatty examples. ([github.com](https://github.com/spf13/cobra?utm_source=openai))  
- Expose explicit flags (e.g., --yes, --interactive, --output=json); don’t rely solely on TTY detection for program logic. See CLI Spec/best-practice posts. ([clispec.dev](https://clispec.dev/?utm_source=openai))  
- If you implement a TUI, use established TUI libraries (Bubble Tea, tview, termbox, etc.) and ensure your program also works in non-interactive scripts (provide equivalent flags/subcommands). See TUI vs CLI articles. ([yorukot.me](https://yorukot.me/blog/before-you-build-a-tui-or-cli-app/?utm_source=openai))

- SQLite FTS5 official docs — explains FTS5 as the SQLite virtual‑table full‑text search module and how virtual tables like FTS5 are used/created (CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE ... USING fts5(...)). Useful for SQL-level usage. ([sqlite.org](https://sqlite.org/fts5.html))

Additional context:
- For other Go SQLite drivers (e.g., mattn/go-sqlite3) you commonly enable FTS5 with a build tag (e.g., go build --tags "fts5"); modernc.org/sqlite uses its own compile/packaging approach so you don’t need the same tag pattern. <summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://pkg.go.dev/gosqlite.org%40v0.10.0/fts`

```text
Yes.

The docs state (exact quote):

"FTS5 is compiled into modernc.org/sqlite/lib (this package's underlying engine) by default — no extension to load. Blank-importing the parent driver is sufficient:"

They then show the usage example:

import (
    "database/sql"
    _ "gosqlite.org"
    "gosqlite.org/fts"
)

So FTS5 is available in the modernc-backed build by default (no separate loadable extension needed); you just blank-import the gosqlite driver and can use gosqlite.org/fts.
```
</details></tool-use>

Summary of what I found for the query "Bing translator unofficial free API no key t3.data.bing.com ttranslatev3 token deprecated 2025"

- Unofficial endpoint exists and is widely used by community projects and scripts. Many open‑source tools call the web endpoint https://www.bing.com/ttranslatev3 (or build requests to bing.com/translator) to get translations. Example usages and troubleshooting threads show people POSTing to /ttranslatev3 and parsing out page variables (token, key, IG, IID) from the translator web page. ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57580445/problems-with-scraping-bing-translations?utm_source=openai))

- The “no key / free” claim is misleading in practice: those unofficial clients typically extract a token/key/IG from the Bing Translator web page (JavaScript variables such as params_AbusePreventionHelper, or data-iid) and include them in the /ttranslatev3 request. In other words, you normally need to scrape the translator page to obtain those transient values — it’s not an officially documented keyless public API. (You can see this pattern in several community scripts and repos that implement automatic extraction and use of IG/IID/token.) ([blog.eswlnk.com](https://blog.eswlnk.com/11913.html?utm_source=openai))

- I did not find any Microsoft documentation that officially announces “ttranslatev3” (or t3.data.bing.com) as a supported public API or that explicitly lists it as deprecated in 2025. Microsoft’s documented Translator APIs are the Azure/Translator endpoints (api.cognitive.microsofttranslator.com and the documented v3/2026 translations), not the bing.com/ttranslatev3 reverse‑engineered endpoint. If you need a supported API, Microsoft points to the official Translator/Azure endpoints and migration guides. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/translator/text-translation/reference/v3/translate?tabs=url&utm_source=openai))

- Related official changes in 2025: Microsoft retired/changed a number of Bing API products (notably the Bing Search APIs) and has been evolving Azure Translator versions (there are migration notes and newer API versions). Those official retirements/changes in 2025 affected many official Bing/Azure search endpoints; they do not, however, constitute a Microsoft announcement specifically deprecating the bing.com/ttranslatev3 reverse‑engineered endpoint. (Because the unofficial endpoint is unsupported, it can be changed or blocked at any time without formal notice.) ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/5492870/deprecation-information-for-userlocation-and-autos?utm_source=openai))

- Practical conclusion / recommendation
  - If you need stable, supported translation access: use Microsoft’s official Translator (Azure Cognitive Services Translator) or another supported API (DeepL, Google Cloud Translate, or self‑hosted LibreTranslate). Those have documented authentication and SLAs. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/translator/text-translation/how-to/migrate-to-v3?utm_source=openai))
  - If you rely on the unofficial bing.com/ttranslatev3 route: expect fragility (tokens change, rate‑limits, IP blocking, legal/ToS risk). It is not a reliable production option and may stop working without notice.
  - If you want, I can: (a) try a live request to /ttranslatev3 from here to see whether it currently responds and which parameters it requires, or (b) show a small example of how community scripts extract token/IG from the translator page — but note I’ll describe the technique only for informational purposes (and recommend using official APIs for production).

I ran searches for: "translate.googleapis.com translate_a single client=gtx rate limit 429 unofficial reliability" and reviewed community posts, Q&A threads, and official docs. Summary of what I found and recommended next steps:

Key findings
- The translate.googleapis.com/translate_a/single?client=gtx call is a widely used but unofficial/undocumented endpoint (community-driven usage, not the Cloud Translation product). ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/57397073/difference-between-the-google-translate-api?utm_source=openai))  
- Some community libraries report that using client=gtx can avoid the web-page token (tk) in some cases, but this is not supported and can lead to other errors (403/captcha) or intermittent failures. ([maxbox6.wordpress.com](https://maxbox6.wordpress.com/2026/02/11/free-google-translator-api-ii/?utm_source=openai))  
- Many users report receiving HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) or other errors when using that endpoint at scale (examples include add-ons and scripts that hit 429 after a burst of requests). This is consistent with the endpoint being rate-limited or otherwise protected. ([ankiweb.net](https://ankiweb.net/shared/info/1536291224?utm_source=openai))  
- For reliable, documented quotas and production use, Google’s official Cloud Translation API (paid / API-key or project-based) is the supported option and has explicit quota/rate-limit documentation. If you need reliability, use the official API. ([docs.cloud.google.com](https://docs.cloud.google.com/translate/quotas?utm_source=openai))  
- Standard mitigations for 429s apply: implement request batching, throttle requests, respect Retry-After if present, and use exponential backoff (with jitter) when retrying — or fail over to the official API / other providers for production reliability. These are common best practices for handling 429s. ([developer.mozilla.org](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Status/429?utm_source=openai))

---

# Verified Go library facts

## 1. SQLite in a pure-Go static binary (modernc + FTS5)
- **modernc.org/sqlite is CGO-free and ships FTS5 compiled in by default.** No build tag, no loadable extension. The `modernc.org/sqlite/lib` package exposes `FTS5_*` constants, and the gosqlite wrapper states verbatim: *"FTS5 is compiled into modernc.org/sqlite/lib … by default — no extension to load."* Current builds bundle SQLite ~3.53.2 (which includes FTS5 in core).
- Contrast: `mattn/go-sqlite3` (CGO) requires `go build -tags "fts5"` and a C toolchain — avoid it for a single static cross-compiled binary.
- **Recommended driver:**
  ```go
  import (
      "database/sql"
      _ "modernc.org/sqlite" // driver name is "sqlite" (NOT "sqlite3")
  )
  db, _ := sql.Open("sqlite", "file:history.db?_pragma=journal_mode(WAL)")
  db.Exec(`CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE history_fts USING fts5(source, translation, notes)`)
  ```
- **Verify at runtime** (cheap self-test on first open, so you can fall back gracefully): `SELECT sqlite_compileoption_used('ENABLE_FTS5')` returns `1`. This is worth doing since FTS5-in-modernc is well-supported but version-dependent — pin the module version in go.mod.
- **Fallback if FTS5 ever fails:** plain `LIKE`/`instr` search on a normal table (adequate for a personal history table of thousands of rows). No need for bbolt.
- Uncertainty flag: modernc FTS5 has been solid for years, but I confirmed it via the wrapper package docs and the exposed lib constants rather than an explicit line in the main README — the runtime `sqlite_compileoption_used` check removes all doubt.

## 2. SQLite vs JSONL for prototype history — recommendation
- **For v1, prefer a JSONL append file + in-memory filter/fuzzy search.** Translation history is append-mostly, low-volume (hundreds–thousands of entries), and a full file scan on startup is microseconds. This eliminates a schema, migrations, FTS5 tokenizer choices, and the WAL/locking surface — big simplicity win for a prototype.
  - Sketch: append one `TranslateResult` JSON object per line to `~/.local/share/translate/history.jsonl`; on TUI open, read all lines into `[]TranslateResult`; fuzzy-rank with `sahilm/fuzzy` (in-memory, same lib Bubbles uses).
- **"Last language pair" is state, not history** — put it in a tiny `state.json`/`state.toml` under `XDG_STATE_HOME`, don't stand up a DB for it.
- **Choose sqlite/FTS5 only if** you want ranked substring/prefix search over a large corpus or SQL querying later. Since modernc+FTS5 is genuinely zero-friction (static, cgo-free), graduating to it later is cheap.
- **Actionable:** define a `Store` interface (`Add(TranslateResult)`, `Search(q) []TranslateResult`, `LastPair()`) and back it with JSONL in v1; swap to sqlite behind the same interface if/when it graduates into dotfiles. This keeps the locked "sqlite w/ FTS" decision as a drop-in upgrade, not a v1 blocker.

## 3. XDG paths on macOS — IMPORTANT CORRECTION to the locked assumption
- **`github.com/adrg/xdg` does NOT default to `~/.config` on macOS.** Its macOS defaults are:
  - `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` → `~/Library/Application Support`
  - `XDG_DATA_HOME`  → `~/Library/Application Support`
  - `XDG_STATE_HOME` → `~/Library/Application Support`
  - `XDG_CACHE_HOME` → `~/Library/Caches`
- It **does honor the env vars** if set (they take precedence over the macOS defaults). So `~/.config` on macOS happens *only if* `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is exported.
- Stdlib `os.UserConfigDir()` **also** returns `~/Library/Application Support` on macOS — so neither adrg nor stdlib gives `~/.config` on macOS by default.
- **The project's stated convention (`~/.config/translate/`, `~/.local/share/…`, `~/.local/state/…`) is Linux-XDG semantics, which macOS libraries deliberately do not follow.** To honor it on macOS you must not rely on defaults.
- **Recommendation:** write a tiny resolver that mirrors freedesktop semantics on all platforms (this is what tools like Neovim/lazygit-style `~/.config` users expect):
  ```go
  func configHome() string {
      if v := os.Getenv("XDG_CONFIG_HOME"); v != "" { return v }
      h, _ := os.UserHomeDir()
      return filepath.Join(h, ".config") // ~/.config on macOS AND Linux
  }
  // same pattern: XDG_DATA_HOME -> ~/.local/share, XDG_STATE_HOME -> ~/.local/state
  ```
  Then `filepath.Join(configHome(), "translate", "config.toml")`, etc., and `os.MkdirAll` the parent on first run.
- If you still want adrg/xdg's ergonomics (its `ConfigFile`, `SearchConfigFile`, `UserDirs`, `Reload()` are nice), you can force the convention by exporting the env vars before its `init`, or by using it only on Linux and your own resolver on macOS — but for a `~/.config`-convention tool, the ~10-line manual resolver is simpler and has no surprising platform branch. adrg/xdg API for reference: `xdg.ConfigFile("translate/config.toml")`, `xdg.DataFile(...)`, `xdg.StateFile(...)`, `xdg.SearchConfigFile(...)`, vars `xdg.ConfigHome`/`xdg.DataHome`/`xdg.StateHome`.

## 4. TOML library — recommendation
- **Use `github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2`.** Idiomatic default in 2026: fastest, actively maintained, targets TOML 1.0/1.1, clean `encoding/json`-style `Marshal`/`Unmarshal` with `toml:"..."` struct tags, and correctly round-trips nested tables and **arrays-of-tables (`[[provider]]`)** in both directions — exactly your config shape.
  ```go
  import "github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2"
  type Provider struct { Name, BaseURL, Model string `toml:"..."` }
  type Config struct {
      Engine    string     `toml:"engine"`
      Providers []Provider `toml:"provider"` // emits/reads [[provider]]
  }
  b, _ := toml.Marshal(cfg)
  _ = toml.Unmarshal(data, &cfg)
  ```
- **Avoid `github.com/pelletier/go-toml` v1** — deprecated in favor of v2.
- **`github.com/BurntSushi/toml`** is fine and stable for *decoding* `[[provider]]` into `[]Provider`, but its encoder has quirks around top-level arrays-of-tables and is less ergonomic for read-modify-write. Since `translate init` will both write and read config, go-toml/v2 is the better fit.

---

### Final recommendations (import paths)
- **SQLite driver:** `modernc.org/sqlite` (cgo-free, driver name `"sqlite"`, FTS5 built in) — but **for v1 use JSONL history** (`~/.local/share/translate/history.jsonl`) + in-memory fuzzy (`github.com/sahilm/fuzzy`) behind a `Store` interface; keep modernc+FTS5 as the drop-in upgrade.
- **XDG paths:** roll a ~10-line `XDG_*`-with-`~/.config` fallback resolver (do **not** rely on `github.com/adrg/xdg` or `os.UserConfigDir` defaults — both map to `~/Library/Application Support` on macOS, breaking your `~/.config` convention).
- **TOML:** `github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2` for marshal+unmarshal of the nested-table / `[[provider]]` config.

Sources: [modernc.org/sqlite](https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite), [modernc.org/sqlite/lib (FTS5 constants)](https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite/lib), [gosqlite fts wrapper (FTS5 default)](https://pkg.go.dev/gosqlite.org%40v0.10.0/fts), [mattn/go-sqlite3 FTS5 build-tag contrast](https://github.com/mattn/go-sqlite3/issues/340), [adrg/xdg README](https://github.com/adrg/xdg), [SQLite FTS5 docs](https://www.sqlite.org/fts5.html), [pelletier/go-toml/v2](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2), [BurntSushi/toml](https://github.com/BurntSushi/toml).

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-09 11:23:41Z)**_

Verified. Final recommendations below.

## 1. Google unofficial endpoint — CONFIRMED, use as free-path default
`GET https://translate.googleapis.com/translate_a/single?client=gtx&sl=<auto|src>&tl=<tgt>&dt=t&q=<urlenc>`

Live response for `sl=auto&tl=es&q=hello world` (with `dt=t&dt=bd&dt=at`):
```
[[["Hola Mundo","hello world",null,null,10]],null,"en",null,null,
 [["hello world",null,[["Hola Mundo",null,true,false,[10]],["hola mundial",null,true,false,[8]]],[[0,11]],"hello world",0,0]],
 0.76348495,[],[["en"],null,[0.76348495],["en"]]]
```
Parsing (nested-array positions are stable across the projects that use this):
- **Full translation** = concatenate `data[0][i][0]` for all i (segment 0 = translated chunk, segment 1 = original chunk). Never assume one segment — long text splits into many.
- **Detected source lang** = `data[2]` (`"en"`). Confidence at `data[8][2][0]` (~0.76).
- **`dt` params are additive and each adds a top-level slot**: `dt=t` (translation, index 0), `dt=bd` = **b**ilingual **d**ictionary block at `data[1]` (per-POS: `[posLabel, [terms...], [[term, [reverseTranslations], score, freq]...], ...]`) — only populated for single words/lemmas, `null` for phrases like "hello world"; `dt=at` = **a**lternate translations at `data[5]`. You can pass multiple `dt=` in one call.
- Recommendation: request `client=gtx&dt=t&dt=bd&dt=at` always; parse index 0 (required), 2 (detect), and opportunistically 1/5 to enrich the Marvin-lite `Alternatives[]`.

Caveats: undocumented/unofficial, can break without notice; community reports **HTTP 429** after bursts and occasional 403/captcha. Treat as best-effort: single request per debounce (the 400ms debounce + cancel-in-flight already throttles well), add exponential backoff+jitter on 429, and fall through to the next engine on any non-200. Set a browser-ish `User-Agent`; `client=gtx` avoids the `tk` token requirement.

Alternative free option — **MyMemory** (CONFIRMED, good secondary): `GET https://api.mymemory.translated.net/get?q=<text>&langpair=en|es`
```json
{"responseData":{"translatedText":"Hola Mundo","match":1},"responseStatus":200,
 "quotaFinished":false,"matches":[{"translation":"...","quality":"...","match":0.9,...}]}
```
Simple flat shape (`responseData.translatedText`, `responseStatus`, `matches[]` for alternatives). Limits: **5,000 chars/day anonymous**, 50,000/day if you add `&de=email@x`; **500 bytes max per `q`**. No auto-detect of source (needs explicit langpair). Recommendation: keep Google unofficial as primary free path (has detect + no daily cap), wire MyMemory as an easy fallback since its shape is trivial. Skip public LibreTranslate instances for v1 — the public ones are flaky/require keys now.

## 2. Bing/Microsoft — DEFER
No documented keyless path. The community `https://www.bing.com/ttranslatev3` route requires scraping transient `IG`/`IID`/`token`/`key` from the translator web page first, then POSTing — fragile, ToS-risky, breaks often. The only supported Microsoft path is Azure Translator (`api.cognitive.microsofttranslator.com`, requires Azure subscription key). Recommendation: **do not include Bing in v1**; the LLM chain + Google + MyMemory cover the free need. Leave an engine interface seam so an Azure-key Bing engine can drop in later.

## 3. Dictionary — use dictionaryapi.dev for v1, local wordlist+Levenshtein for fuzzy
Free Dictionary API (CONFIRMED): `GET https://api.dictionaryapi.dev/api/v2/entries/en/<word>` → array of entries:
```
word, phonetic, phonetics[]{text,audio}, meanings[]{ partOfSpeech,
  definitions[]{definition, example?, synonyms[], antonyms[]}, synonyms[], antonyms[] },
license{name,url}, sourceUrls[]
```
On miss it returns **HTTP 404** (confirmed for `helllo`) with a `{title,message,resolution}` body — **no suggestions/nearest-word field**. So the API cannot power fuzzy itself.

Fuzzy strategy recommendation: on 404, resolve nearest headword **locally** — ship a bundled English wordlist (e.g. SCOWL/`words` ~50–100k, or the dictionaryapi word set) and rank candidates by Levenshtein/Damerau distance (Go: `github.com/agnivade/levenshtein` or `agext/levenshtein`), optionally pre-filter by a trigram/prefix index for speed, then re-query the API with the top suggestion (mirrors the CLI's cobra "did you mean" UX). This keeps it offline-capable and deterministic.

Wiktionary REST (`https://en.wiktionary.org/api/rest_v1/page/definition/<word>`) — object keyed by language code (`"en"`, `"fr"`, …), each value an array of `{partOfSpeech, language, definitions[]{definition, parsedExamples[], examples[]}}`. It's **multi-language** (good later) but definitions contain HTML markup needing stripping, and it's heavier to parse. Recommendation: **v1 = dictionaryapi.dev** (clean JSON, English, synonyms/antonyms/phonetics/audio out of the box); keep Wiktionary REST as a documented option for multi-language dictionary in a later version.

## 4. Offline language detection in Go — recommend whatlanggo for the prototype
- `github.com/abadojack/whatlanggo` — light, pure-Go, fast, no large model download, trivial API (`whatlang.Detect(text)` → lang + script + confidence). Best fit for a prototype and for the "auto" source when Google is offline.
- `github.com/pemistahl/lingua-go` — most accurate on short/single-word/mixed text, but heavy (large embedded n-gram models, bigger binary, slower init) — matters for a "single static binary" goal.

Recommendation: **start with whatlanggo** (`github.com/abadojack/whatlanggo`) for binary size + speed; note lingua-go as an upgrade path behind the same internal `Detector` interface if short-text accuracy proves insufficient. In practice detection is mostly a fallback since Google returns `data[2]` and the LLM returns `DetectedSource` in the structured result — so keeping detection light is the right call.

Note: I did not write a plan file — per instructions these findings are returned directly as my output.

Sources: [Free Dictionary API](https://dictionaryapi.dev/), [Wiktionary REST](https://en.wiktionary.org/api/rest_v1/), [MyMemory limits](https://mymemory.translated.net/doc/usagelimits.php), [lingua-go](https://github.com/pemistahl/lingua-go), [whatlanggo](https://github.com/abadojack/whatlanggo), [Azure Translator v3](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/translator/text-translation/reference/v3/translate).

I'm pulling together the final answer with concrete details—confirmed release dates, module paths, and version numbers for the Charm libraries as of mid-2026, starting with Bubble Tea v2 and its dependencies. Lip Gloss v2 keeps the styling API intact with color support via hex and ANSI codes, and handles dark mode detection through tea.BackgroundColorMsg rather than a global renderer. huh v2 (v2.0.3) builds on the updated Bubble Tea and Lip Gloss, offering form components like Input, Select, and Confirm that can run standalone or embed as a tea.Model. For streaming data, I'm using self-resubscribing commands that read from channels and re-issue themselves in Update. One important gotcha with stdin: I need to check if it's a terminal using `term.IsTerminal()` or stat for the character device flag—if stdin is piped or args are present, skip the TUI and run one-shot mode instead, since Bubble Tea takes over the TTY.

The key integration detail for huh v2 is that Form.View() still returns a string, so in the parent model's View() I wrap it with tea.NewView to compose it into the v2 rendering pipeline. There's a significant caveat here: most existing tutorials and LLM training data show v1 patterns with `View() string` and `tea.KeyMsg`, so code generation will likely default to v1 and produce incompatible code. Since this is greenfield work in 2026, v2 is the current idiomatic choice, though v1 (v1.3.10) remains maintained if they prefer the more-documented path. I should surface this divergence clearly so the implementer knows to be explicit about v2 when working with code generation tools.

Now I'm sketching out the concrete message and command structure for the debounce-plus-cancel-plus-stream combination that's central to this request. Finishing the goroutine to stream tokens into the channel while respecting context cancellation, then returning both the chunk-waiting command and a done-waiting command. For the model mutations, I'll keep them inline in the Update method since that's idiomatic for Bubble Tea—setting cancel and stream directly on m before returning. Now I need to handle the layout with JoinHorizontal and JoinVertical, responding to window size changes.

Handling window resize by calculating proportions for the left panel (two-thirds width) and right panel, then setting dimensions on the textarea, viewport, and history list. The View method stacks the input and result vertically on the left, places the history list on the right, and joins them horizontally with a footer below.

For the CLI entry point, I'm checking whether input is piped or if text was passed as arguments, routing to a one-shot translation mode, or falling back to the interactive TUI.

All facts confirmed. Here is the verified research report.

---

# Charm TUI stack — verified current patterns (July 2026)

## ⚠️ Headline finding: the stack moved to v2 + new module paths (Feb 2026)

Charm shipped the **v2 family out of beta on ~2026-02-23** and moved to a **`charm.land/*/v2` vanity module path** (not `github.com/charmbracelet/...`). For a greenfield project in July 2026 this is the current idiomatic stack. Pin these:

| Lib | Import path | Version | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bubble Tea | `charm.land/bubbletea/v2` | v2.0.8 | Elm runtime (Model/Update/View, Cmd/Msg) |
| Bubbles | `charm.land/bubbles/v2` | v2.0.x | components (subpackages below) |
| Lip Gloss | `charm.land/lipgloss/v2` | v2.0.5 | styling + layout |
| huh | `charm.land/huh/v2` | v2.0.3 | forms; built on tea v2 + lipgloss v2 |

Requires **Go 1.25**. cobra is unaffected (still `github.com/spf13/cobra`).

**Biggest caveat for you:** almost all tutorials, blog posts, and — critically — LLM-generated code (including the copilot-proxy models you plan to call) default to **v1** (`github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea`, `View() string`, `tea.KeyMsg`). v1 code will **not compile against v2**. Be explicit in prompts/CLAUDE.md that this is a v2 `charm.land/*` project. v1 is still maintained (bubbletea v1.3.10) if you'd rather ride the better-documented path, but then use v1 paths consistently.

### v2 breaking changes that matter here
- `View() tea.View` (was `string`) → wrap with `tea.NewView("...")`.
- Keys: `tea.KeyPressMsg` (was `tea.KeyMsg`); fields `.Code`, `.Text` (string, was `.Runes`), `.Mod` (was `.Alt`). `.String()` still works for matching, and `bubbles/v2/key` still works.
- Cursor is declarative via the `tea.View.Cursor` field (imperative cursor cmds removed).
- `tea.RequestWindowSize` (renamed from `tea.WindowSize`). `tea.WindowSizeMsg` unchanged.
- `Init() tea.Cmd` and `Update(tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd)` **unchanged**. `tea.NewProgram(m, tea.WithAltScreen()).Run()`, `tea.Batch`, `tea.Sequence`, `tea.Tick`, `tea.Quit` **unchanged**.

## 1. Components — all present

**Bubbles v2** subpackages (import `charm.land/bubbles/v2/<pkg>`): `textarea`, `textinput`, `viewport`, `list`, `spinner`, `help`, `key`, plus `table`, `progress`, `paginator`, `cursor`, `filepicker`, `timer`, `stopwatch`. Everything you listed exists.

**huh v2** field constructors: `NewInput`, `NewText`, `NewSelect[T]`, `NewMultiSelect[T]`, `NewConfirm`, grouped via `NewGroup`/`NewForm`. Runs **both ways**:
- Standalone wizard: `err := form.Run()` (blocking) — ideal for `translate init`.
- Embedded: `huh.Form` **is a `tea.Model`** — call `form.Init/Update/View`, check `m.form.State == huh.StateCompleted`. In a v2 parent, `form.View()` returns a string, so wrap it: `return tea.NewView(m.form.View())`.

For the probing init wizard, run huh **standalone** (`form.Run()`) and do the provider HTTP probes either before the form (show results as `Description()` on options) or between groups — simplest is standalone, not embedded.

## 2. Debounce + cancel + stream skeleton (the core)

All three keyed off **one monotonic `seq` counter**. Every keystroke bumps `seq`, which (a) invalidates the pending debounce tick, (b) is the cancel signal for the in-flight request, and (c) stamps every stream msg so stale/cancelled chunks are dropped in O(1). This is exactly the pattern in Charm's official `examples/debounce` (tag counter + `tea.Tick`) fused with `examples/realtime` (self-resubscribing channel reader).

```go
import (
    "context"; "time"
    tea "charm.land/bubbletea/v2"
    "charm.land/bubbles/v2/textarea"
    "charm.land/bubbles/v2/viewport"
)

type debounceMsg  struct{ seq int }
type chunkMsg      struct{ seq int; text string }
type doneMsg       struct{ seq int; res TranslateResult; err error }

type model struct {
    ta         textarea.Model
    vp         viewport.Model
    seq        int                 // generation / request id
    cancel     context.CancelFunc  // cancels in-flight engine call
    stream     chan chunkMsg       // engine -> UI
    debounceOn bool
    result     string
}

func (m model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    switch msg := msg.(type) {

    case tea.KeyPressMsg:
        if msg.String() == "enter" {           // immediate translate
            return m.launch()                  // (bumps seq, cancels, kicks goroutine)
        }
        var cmd tea.Cmd
        m.ta, cmd = m.ta.Update(msg)
        if m.debounceOn {
            m.seq++                             // (a) invalidate pending tick
            if m.cancel != nil { m.cancel() }   // (b) cancel in-flight
            return m, tea.Batch(cmd, arm(m.seq))
        }
        return m, cmd

    case debounceMsg:                           // 400ms of idle elapsed?
        if msg.seq != m.seq { return m, nil }   // newer keystroke arrived -> ignore
        return m.launch()

    case chunkMsg:
        if msg.seq != m.seq { return m, nil }   // (c) stale/cancelled -> drop
        m.result += msg.text
        m.vp.SetContent(m.result)
        return m, m.waitChunk(m.seq)            // re-subscribe for next token

    case doneMsg:
        if msg.seq != m.seq { return m, nil }
        // render final structured TranslateResult (alternatives, notes, confidence)
        return m, nil
    }
    return m, nil
}

func arm(seq int) tea.Cmd {                     // debounce timer
    return tea.Tick(400*time.Millisecond, func(time.Time) tea.Msg {
        return debounceMsg{seq: seq}
    })
}

// launch: bump seq, make a fresh context+channel, start streaming goroutine.
func (m model) launch() (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {
    m.seq++
    if m.cancel != nil { m.cancel() }
    ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
    ch := make(chan chunkMsg, 32)
    m.cancel, m.stream, m.result = cancel, ch, ""
    seq, text := m.seq, m.ta.Value()

    go func() {                                 // engine respects ctx; OpenAI SSE or google call
        defer close(ch)
        engine.Stream(ctx, text, func(tok string) {
            select {
            case ch <- chunkMsg{seq: seq, text: tok}:
            case <-ctx.Done():                  // cancelled: stop feeding
            }
        })
    }()
    return m, m.waitChunk(seq)
}

func (m model) waitChunk(seq int) tea.Cmd {     // self-resubscribing reader (realtime pattern)
    ch := m.stream
    return func() tea.Msg {
        c, ok := <-ch
        if !ok { return doneMsg{seq: seq} }     // channel closed = stream finished
        return c
    }
}
```

Notes / correctness:
- **`seq` is the single source of truth.** You do not even need to check `ctx` in the UI thread — the `msg.seq != m.seq` guard makes cancelled results unrenderable. `ctx` cancellation is purely to stop wasted network/LLM work.
- `tea.Tick` fires exactly once per call, so one armed tick per keystroke; the guard collapses the storm to the last one. This is the sanctioned Charm idiom (their comment: "if the tag in the Msg matches current tag… otherwise we simply ignore the inbound message").
- Non-streaming engines (google free API, dictionary) just skip `chunkMsg` and return a single `doneMsg{seq, res}` from a plain `tea.Cmd`.
- Buffer the channel (e.g. 32) so a fast LLM stream doesn't block on a slow render frame.

## 3. Two-pane responsive layout (Lip Gloss v2)

`NewStyle`, `JoinHorizontal`, `JoinVertical`, `Place`, `Width/Height`, `Border` all survive into v2. Recompute widths on `tea.WindowSizeMsg`; compose with joins.

```go
import lg "charm.land/lipgloss/v2"

case tea.WindowSizeMsg:
    m.width, m.height = msg.Width, msg.Height
    footerH := 1
    leftW  := msg.Width * 2 / 3
    rightW := msg.Width - leftW
    bodyH  := msg.Height - footerH
    inputH := 4
    m.ta.SetWidth(leftW-2);  m.ta.SetHeight(inputH)
    m.vp.Width = leftW - 2;  m.vp.Height = bodyH - inputH - 2
    m.hist.SetSize(rightW-2, bodyH)

func (m model) View() tea.View {
    left := lg.JoinVertical(lg.Left,
        boxStyle.Render(m.ta.View()),
        boxStyle.Render(m.vp.View()))
    right  := boxStyle.Width(m.rightW).Render(m.hist.View())
    body   := lg.JoinHorizontal(lg.Top, left, right)
    footer := footerStyle.Render(m.help.View(m.keys))
    return tea.NewView(lg.JoinVertical(lg.Left, body, footer))
}
```

Color in v2: no global renderer. Use `lg.Color("#7D56F4")` / ANSI codes; for light/dark adaptivity handle `tea.BackgroundColorMsg` (send `tea.RequestBackgroundColor`, use `msg.IsDark()`) inside the TUI, or `lipgloss.HasDarkBackground(os.Stdin, os.Stdout)` standalone. A `compat` package offers v1-style `AdaptiveColor` but Charm explicitly discourages it for new code.

## 4. stdin-pipe vs TTY gotcha — real, handle it

Bubble Tea takes over the TTY via a cancelreader; if stdin is a pipe the interactive loop gets immediate EOF/garbage. **Branch before launching.** Use `golang.org/x/term.IsTerminal(int(os.Stdin.Fd()))` (preferred over `mattn/go-isatty`; or `os.Stdin.Stat()` + `os.ModeCharDevice`).

```go
func run(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    switch {
    case len(args) > 0:                                   // translate "text"
        return oneShot(strings.Join(args, " "))
    case !term.IsTerminal(int(os.Stdin.Fd())):            // echo x | translate
        b, _ := io.ReadAll(os.Stdin)
        return oneShot(strings.TrimSpace(string(b)))
    default:                                              // bare, interactive TTY
        _, err := tea.NewProgram(newModel(), tea.WithAltScreen()).Run()
        return err
    }
}
```

Also gate color/ANSI on **stdout** being a TTY for the one-shot path (so `translate x | pcopy` stays clean) — Lip Gloss v2 auto-downsamples when you emit via its writer helpers or when output isn't a TTY. cobra's "did you mean" suggestions are on by default (`SuggestionsMinimumDistance`), satisfying the subcommand-typo requirement; language-name fuzzing (chinees→zh) is your own resolver, not cobra.

---

### Quick recommendation summary
- Adopt the **v2 `charm.land/*` stack** (tea 2.0.8, bubbles 2.0.x, lipgloss 2.0.5, huh 2.0.3, Go 1.25); document it loudly so v1-trained code-gen doesn't poison the build.
- **One `seq` counter** unifies debounce-ignore, cancel, and stale-stream-drop — mirror Charm's `debounce` + `realtime` examples. Keep `context.Context`/`cancel` on the model only to stop wasted engine work.
- huh: **standalone `form.Run()`** for `translate init`; embed-as-`tea.Model` only if you later fold it into the TUI.
- Layout via `JoinHorizontal/JoinVertical` recomputed on `WindowSizeMsg`; `View()` returns `tea.NewView(...)`.
- Detect `!IsTerminal(stdin)` (or args present) → one-shot; else TUI.

### Sources
- https://charm.land/blog/v2/ — v2 out-of-beta announcement (2026-02-23)
- https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/blob/main/UPGRADE_GUIDE_V2.md — v2 breaking changes (`View() tea.View`, `KeyPressMsg`, `RequestWindowSize`)
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/main/examples/debounce/main.go — canonical tag/seq + `tea.Tick` debounce
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/main/examples/realtime/main.go — self-resubscribing channel-reader Cmd (streaming)
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/bubbles/main/go.mod — `module charm.land/bubbles/v2`, requires tea v2.0.8, lipgloss v2.0.5
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/lipgloss/main/go.mod — `module charm.land/lipgloss/v2`
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/huh/main/go.mod — `module charm.land/huh/v2`, on tea v2 + lipgloss v2
- https://pkg.go.dev/charm.land/huh/v2 — huh v2 API (Form is a tea.Model, StateCompleted)
- https://golang.org/x/term — `IsTerminal(int(fd))`

# Verified Go library facts

## 1. SQLite in a pure-Go static binary (modernc + FTS5)
- **modernc.org/sqlite is CGO-free and ships FTS5 compiled in by default.** No build tag, no loadable extension. The `modernc.org/sqlite/lib` package exposes `FTS5_*` constants, and the gosqlite wrapper states verbatim: *"FTS5 is compiled into modernc.org/sqlite/lib … by default — no extension to load."* Current builds bundle SQLite ~3.53.2 (which includes FTS5 in core).
- Contrast: `mattn/go-sqlite3` (CGO) requires `go build -tags "fts5"` and a C toolchain — avoid it for a single static cross-compiled binary.
- **Recommended driver:**
  ```go
  import (
      "database/sql"
      _ "modernc.org/sqlite" // driver name is "sqlite" (NOT "sqlite3")
  )
  db, _ := sql.Open("sqlite", "file:history.db?_pragma=journal_mode(WAL)")
  db.Exec(`CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE history_fts USING fts5(source, translation, notes)`)
  ```
- **Verify at runtime** (cheap self-test on first open, so you can fall back gracefully): `SELECT sqlite_compileoption_used('ENABLE_FTS5')` returns `1`. This is worth doing since FTS5-in-modernc is well-supported but version-dependent — pin the module version in go.mod.
- **Fallback if FTS5 ever fails:** plain `LIKE`/`instr` search on a normal table (adequate for a personal history table of thousands of rows). No need for bbolt.
- Uncertainty flag: modernc FTS5 has been solid for years, but I confirmed it via the wrapper package docs and the exposed lib constants rather than an explicit line in the main README — the runtime `sqlite_compileoption_used` check removes all doubt.

## 2. SQLite vs JSONL for prototype history — recommendation
- **For v1, prefer a JSONL append file + in-memory filter/fuzzy search.** Translation history is append-mostly, low-volume (hundreds–thousands of entries), and a full file scan on startup is microseconds. This eliminates a schema, migrations, FTS5 tokenizer choices, and the WAL/locking surface — big simplicity win for a prototype.
  - Sketch: append one `TranslateResult` JSON object per line to `~/.local/share/translate/history.jsonl`; on TUI open, read all lines into `[]TranslateResult`; fuzzy-rank with `sahilm/fuzzy` (in-memory, same lib Bubbles uses).
- **"Last language pair" is state, not history** — put it in a tiny `state.json`/`state.toml` under `XDG_STATE_HOME`, don't stand up a DB for it.
- **Choose sqlite/FTS5 only if** you want ranked substring/prefix search over a large corpus or SQL querying later. Since modernc+FTS5 is genuinely zero-friction (static, cgo-free), graduating to it later is cheap.
- **Actionable:** define a `Store` interface (`Add(TranslateResult)`, `Search(q) []TranslateResult`, `LastPair()`) and back it with JSONL in v1; swap to sqlite behind the same interface if/when it graduates into dotfiles. This keeps the locked "sqlite w/ FTS" decision as a drop-in upgrade, not a v1 blocker.

## 3. XDG paths on macOS — IMPORTANT CORRECTION to the locked assumption
- **`github.com/adrg/xdg` does NOT default to `~/.config` on macOS.** Its macOS defaults are:
  - `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` → `~/Library/Application Support`
  - `XDG_DATA_HOME`  → `~/Library/Application Support`
  - `XDG_STATE_HOME` → `~/Library/Application Support`
  - `XDG_CACHE_HOME` → `~/Library/Caches`
- It **does honor the env vars** if set (they take precedence over the macOS defaults). So `~/.config` on macOS happens *only if* `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is exported.
- Stdlib `os.UserConfigDir()` **also** returns `~/Library/Application Support` on macOS — so neither adrg nor stdlib gives `~/.config` on macOS by default.
- **The project's stated convention (`~/.config/translate/`, `~/.local/share/…`, `~/.local/state/…`) is Linux-XDG semantics, which macOS libraries deliberately do not follow.** To honor it on macOS you must not rely on defaults.
- **Recommendation:** write a tiny resolver that mirrors freedesktop semantics on all platforms (this is what tools like Neovim/lazygit-style `~/.config` users expect):
  ```go
  func configHome() string {
      if v := os.Getenv("XDG_CONFIG_HOME"); v != "" { return v }
      h, _ := os.UserHomeDir()
      return filepath.Join(h, ".config") // ~/.config on macOS AND Linux
  }
  // same pattern: XDG_DATA_HOME -> ~/.local/share, XDG_STATE_HOME -> ~/.local/state
  ```
  Then `filepath.Join(configHome(), "translate", "config.toml")`, etc., and `os.MkdirAll` the parent on first run.
- If you still want adrg/xdg's ergonomics (its `ConfigFile`, `SearchConfigFile`, `UserDirs`, `Reload()` are nice), you can force the convention by exporting the env vars before its `init`, or by using it only on Linux and your own resolver on macOS — but for a `~/.config`-convention tool, the ~10-line manual resolver is simpler and has no surprising platform branch. adrg/xdg API for reference: `xdg.ConfigFile("translate/config.toml")`, `xdg.DataFile(...)`, `xdg.StateFile(...)`, `xdg.SearchConfigFile(...)`, vars `xdg.ConfigHome`/`xdg.DataHome`/`xdg.StateHome`.

## 4. TOML library — recommendation
- **Use `github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2`.** Idiomatic default in 2026: fastest, actively maintained, targets TOML 1.0/1.1, clean `encoding/json`-style `Marshal`/`Unmarshal` with `toml:"..."` struct tags, and correctly round-trips nested tables and **arrays-of-tables (`[[provider]]`)** in both directions — exactly your config shape.
  ```go
  import "github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2"
  type Provider struct { Name, BaseURL, Model string `toml:"..."` }
  type Config struct {
      Engine    string     `toml:"engine"`
      Providers []Provider `toml:"provider"` // emits/reads [[provider]]
  }
  b, _ := toml.Marshal(cfg)
  _ = toml.Unmarshal(data, &cfg)
  ```
- **Avoid `github.com/pelletier/go-toml` v1** — deprecated in favor of v2.
- **`github.com/BurntSushi/toml`** is fine and stable for *decoding* `[[provider]]` into `[]Provider`, but its encoder has quirks around top-level arrays-of-tables and is less ergonomic for read-modify-write. Since `translate init` will both write and read config, go-toml/v2 is the better fit.

---

### Final recommendations (import paths)
- **SQLite driver:** `modernc.org/sqlite` (cgo-free, driver name `"sqlite"`, FTS5 built in) — but **for v1 use JSONL history** (`~/.local/share/translate/history.jsonl`) + in-memory fuzzy (`github.com/sahilm/fuzzy`) behind a `Store` interface; keep modernc+FTS5 as the drop-in upgrade.
- **XDG paths:** roll a ~10-line `XDG_*`-with-`~/.config` fallback resolver (do **not** rely on `github.com/adrg/xdg` or `os.UserConfigDir` defaults — both map to `~/Library/Application Support` on macOS, breaking your `~/.config` convention).
- **TOML:** `github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2` for marshal+unmarshal of the nested-table / `[[provider]]` config.

Sources: [modernc.org/sqlite](https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite), [modernc.org/sqlite/lib (FTS5 constants)](https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite/lib), [gosqlite fts wrapper (FTS5 default)](https://pkg.go.dev/gosqlite.org%40v0.10.0/fts), [mattn/go-sqlite3 FTS5 build-tag contrast](https://github.com/mattn/go-sqlite3/issues/340), [adrg/xdg README](https://github.com/adrg/xdg), [SQLite FTS5 docs](https://www.sqlite.org/fts5.html), [pelletier/go-toml/v2](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2), [BurntSushi/toml](https://github.com/BurntSushi/toml).
